pages.title: Depth and Lefschetz theorems
pages.html:
XIV Depth and Lefschetz theorems in étale cohomology

by Mme M. Raynaud1

1cm In n° 1, we define a notion of "étale depth" which is the analogue in étale cohomology of the notion of depth studied in III, in the cohomology of coherent sheaves. After a technical part, we prove in n° 4 some "Lefschetz theorems", the central theorem being 4.2. Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(Y\) a closed part of \(X\), \(U\) the complementary open set \(X-Y\) and \(F\) an abelian sheaf on \(X\), for the étale topology; in a general manner the aim of the Lefschetz theorems is to show that, if \(F\) satisfies certain local conditions on \(U\), expressible in terms of étale depth at the points of \(U\), then, under certain additional conditions of a global nature on \(U\) (for example \(U\) affine), the natural map of the étale cohomology groups

\[ \operatorname{H}^i(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i(Y, F{|Y}) \]

is an isomorphism for values \(i<n\), where \(n\) is a certain explicit integer. By taking for \(F\) a constant sheaf, one thus obtains conditions for \(\pi_0(X)\) to be equal to \(\pi_0(Y)\) and conditions for the abelianized fundamental groups of \(X\) and \(Y\) to be the same. In n° 5, the introduction of a notion of "geometric depth" allows us to give useful special cases of the Lefschetz theorems (5.7). Finally in n° 6, we point out some conjectures, concerning in particular "non-commutative" variants of the theorems obtained.

pages.title: Cohomological and homotopic depth
pages.html:
1 Cohomological and homotopic depth

1.0

Let us fix the following notation. Let \(X\) be a scheme1, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\), \(U\) the complementary open set and \(i:Y=X-U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical immersion. Let \(\underline{\Gamma}_Y\) be the functor which, to an abelian sheaf on \(X\), associates the "sheaf of sections with support in \(Y\)", that is to say \(\underline{\Gamma}_Y=i_*i^!\) (cf. SGA 4 IV 3.8 and VIII 6.6), and \(\Gamma_Y\) the functor \(\Gamma\cdot \underline{\Gamma}_Y\) (where \(\Gamma\) is the "global sections" functor). Consider the derived category \(D^+(X)\) and the derived functor \(\operatorname{R}\underline{\Gamma}_Y\) (resp. \(\operatorname{R}\Gamma_Y\)) of \(\underline{\Gamma}_Y\) (resp. of \(\Gamma_Y\)) (cf. [3]). Given a complex of abelian sheaves \(F\) on \(X\), with degrees bounded below, one can regard it as an element of \(D^+(X)\); we shall then denote by \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F)\) the \(p\)-th cohomology sheaf of \(\operatorname{R}\underline{\Gamma}_Y(F)\) and by \(\operatorname{H}_Y^p(X, F)\) the \(p\)-th cohomology group of \(\operatorname{R}\Gamma_Y(F)\). The results of (SGA 4 V 4.3 and 4.4) extend trivially to \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F)\) and \(\operatorname{H}_Y^p(X, F)\).

Proposition 1.1

Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\), \(U\) the complementary open set and \(i:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical immersion. Let \(F\) denote either a sheaf of sets on \(X\), or a sheaf of groups on \(X\), or a complex of abelian sheaves on \(X\), with degrees bounded below. Let us fix the following notation: if \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is a morphism, \(U'\) and \(F'\) denote the inverse images of \(U\) and \(F\) on \(X'\); furthermore, if \(\overline{y}\) is a geometric point of \(X\), \(\overline{X}\) denotes the strict localization of \(X\) at \(\overline{y}\) and \(\overline{U}\) and \(\overline{F}\) the inverse images of \(U\) and \(F\) in \(\overline{X}\).

\(1^\circ)\) Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets on \(X\) and \(n\) an integer \(\leqslant 2\); then the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) The canonical morphism

\[ F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*i^*F \]

is injective if \(n\geqslant 1\), bijective if \(n\geqslant 2\).

(ii) For every scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(X', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(U', F') \]

is injective if \(n\geqslant 1\), bijective if \(n\geqslant 2\).

Suppose moreover that the open set \(U\) is retrocompact in \(X\); then the preceding conditions are equivalent to the following:

(iii) For every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(\overline{X}, \overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(\overline{U}, \overline{F}) \]

is injective if \(n\geqslant 1\), and bijective if \(n\geqslant 2\).

\(2^\circ)\) Let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups on \(X\) and \(n\) an integer \(\leqslant 3\); then the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) The canonical morphism

\[ F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*i^*F \]

is injective if \(n\geqslant 1\), bijective if \(n\geqslant 2\), and if \(n\geqslant 3\), in addition to the preceding conditions, the sheaf of pointed sets \(\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F)\) is zero.

(ii) For every scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(X', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(U', F') \]

is injective if \(n\geqslant 1\), bijective if \(n\geqslant 2\), and moreover the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^1(X', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^1(U', F') \]

is injective if \(n\geqslant 2\), bijective if \(n\geqslant 3\).

(ii bis) Identical to (ii), except in the case \(n=2\) where one only assumes \(\operatorname{H}^0(X', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(U', F')\) bijective.

Suppose moreover that \(U\) is retrocompact in \(X\); then the preceding conditions are also equivalent to the following:

(iii) For every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(\overline{X}, \overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(\overline{U}, \overline{F}) \]

is injective if \(n\geqslant 1\), bijective if \(n\geqslant 2\); finally if \(n\geqslant 3\), in addition to the preceding conditions, \(\operatorname{H}^1(\overline{U}, \overline{F})\) is zero.

\(3^\circ)\) Let \(F\) be a complex of abelian sheaves, with degrees bounded below, and \(n\) an integer; then the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) One has \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F)=0\) for \(p<n\) (cf. 1.0).

(ii) For every scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^p(X', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^p(U', F') \]

is bijective for \(p<n-1\), injective for \(p=n-1\).

Suppose \(U\) retrocompact in \(X\); then the preceding conditions are also equivalent to the following:

(iii) For every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^p(\overline{X}, \overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^p(\overline{U}, \overline{F}) \]

is bijective for \(p<n-1\), injective for \(p=n-1\).

In the case where \(F\) is an abelian sheaf and \(n\geqslant 2\), conditions (i) and (ii) are also equivalent to the following:

(ii bis) For every scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^p(X', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^p(U', F') \]

is bijective for \(p<n-1\).

Proof▾

\(1^\circ)\) It is clear that (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (ii). Let us show that, if \(U\) is retrocompact in \(X\), (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (iii). Indeed, (i) amounts to saying that, for every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(X\), the morphism \(F_{\overline{y}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (i_*i^*F)_{\overline{y}}\) is injective if \(n\leqslant 1\) and bijective if \(n\leqslant 2\) (SGA 4 VIII 3.6). Since this morphism is in any case bijective when \(\overline{y}\) is a geometric point of \(U\), one may restrict to the geometric points \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y\). Now it follows from the fact that \(i\) is quasi-compact and from (SGA 4 VIII 5.3) that the morphism

\[ F_{\overline{y}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (i_*i^*F)_{\overline{y}} \]

is canonically identified with the morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(\overline{X}, \overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(\overline{U}, \overline{F}), \]

whence the equivalence of (i) and (iii).

\(2^\circ)\) (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). The assertions on \(\operatorname{H}^0\) follow from \(1^\circ)\). Let then \(i'\) be the canonical immersion of \(U'\) in \(X'\); the assertions on \(\operatorname{H}^1\) follow from the exact sequence (SGA 4 XII 3.2)

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^1(X', i'_*i'^*F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^1(U', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(X', \operatorname{R}^1i'_*(i'^*F')). \]

(ii bis) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i). By \(1^\circ)\), it suffices to show that, for \(n\geqslant 3\), one has \(\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F)=0\). Now \(\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F)\) is the sheaf associated to the presheaf \(X'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \operatorname{H}^1(U', F')\), that is to say, by hypothesis, the sheaf associated to the presheaf \(X'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \operatorname{H}^1(X', F')\), which is zero.

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (iii). Taking \(1^\circ)\) into account, the only thing that remains to be seen is that the relation \(\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F)=0\) is equivalent to the fact that \(\operatorname{H}^1(\overline{U}, \overline{F})=0\) for every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y\). Since \(\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F))\) is zero outside \(Y\), it amounts to the same to say that \(\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F)=0\) or that \((\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F))_{\overline{y}}=0\) for every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y\). It then suffices to note that, \(i\) being quasi-compact, one has \((\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F))_{\overline{y}}=\operatorname{H}^1(\overline{U}, \overline{F})\) (SGA 4 VIII 5.3).

\(3^\circ)\) (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). Let \(X'\) be a scheme étale over \(X\); one has the exact sequence (SGA 4 V 4.5)

\[ \tag{*} {\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^p_{Y'}(X', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^p(X', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^p(U', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}}; \]

hence (ii) is equivalent to \(H^p_{Y'}(X', F')=0\) for \(p<n\) and for every scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\). Consider then the spectral sequence

\[ \operatorname{E}_2^{pq}=\operatorname{H}^p(X', {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^q(F))\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_{Y'}^*(X', F'); \]

by hypothesis, \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^q(F)=0\) for \(q<n\), whence \(\operatorname{E}_2^{pq}=0\) for \(p+q<n\) and consequently \(\operatorname{H}_{Y'}^p(X', F')=0\) for \(p<n\).

(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i). The sheaf \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F)\) is associated to the presheaf \(X'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \operatorname{H}_{Y'}^p(X', F')\); since we have already remarked that (ii) is equivalent to the relation \(\operatorname{H}_{Y'}^p(X', F')=0\) for \(p<n\) and for every scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\), one indeed has \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F)=0\) for \(p<n\).

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (iii). The sheaves \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F)\) are concentrated on \(Y\); consequently it amounts to the same to say that \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F)=0\) or to say that, for every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y\), the stalk \(({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F))_{\overline{y}}\) is zero. Now, \(i\) being quasi-compact, one deduces from (SGA 4 VIII 5.2) that one has \(({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F))_{\overline{y}}={\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_{\overline{Y}}^p(\overline{X}, \overline{F})\). The equivalence of (i) and (iii) follows, taking into account the analogue on \(\overline{X}\) of the exact sequence (*).

(ii bis) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii) in the case where \(F\) is an abelian sheaf. The only thing that remains to be shown is that \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^{n-1}(F)=0\). Now, for \(n>2\), the sheaf \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^{n-1}(F)\) is associated to the presheaf \(X'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \operatorname{H}^{n-2}(U', F')=\operatorname{H}^{n-2}(X', F')\) hence is indeed zero. The case \(n=2\) follows from the fact that \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^1(F)\) is the cokernel of the morphism \(F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*i^*F\).

Q.E.D.
Definition 1.2

The notation is that of 1.1. One says that \(F\) is of étale \(Y\)-depth \(\geqslant n\) and one writes

\[ \operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant n \]

if \(F\) satisfies the equivalent conditions (i) and (ii) of 1.1. If \(F\) is a complex of abelian sheaves, one calls the étale \(Y\)-depth of \(F\) the supremum of the \(n\) for which \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant n\); one will use the same notation if \(F\) is a sheaf of sets resp. of not necessarily commutative groups (so that one then has \(0\leqslant \operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\leqslant 2\) resp. \(0\leqslant \operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\leqslant 3\), when the context does not allow confusion as to which of the three variants considered here is being used).

If \(\mathbf{L}\) is a set of prime numbers, one says that \(X\) is of étale \(Y\)-depth for \(\mathbf{L} \geqslant n\) and one writes

\[ \operatorname{prof}_Y^{\mathbf{L}}(X)\geqslant n \]

if, for every constant sheaf of the form \(\mathbf{Z}/\ell\mathbf{Z}\) with \(\ell\in \mathbf{L}\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(\mathbf{Z}/\ell\mathbf{Z})\geqslant n\). One defines in the obvious way the étale \(Y\)-depth for \(\mathbf{L}\) of \(X\). If \(\mathbf{L}=\mathbf{P}\), the set of all prime numbers, and if there is no risk of confusion with the notation of (EGA IV 5.7.1) (relative to the case where \(F=\mathscr{O}_X\)), one omits \(\mathbf{L}\) in the notation; otherwise one writes \(\operatorname{prof} {\textup{ét}}(X)\).

Finally one says that \(X\) is of homotopic \(Y\)-depth for \(\mathbf{L} \geqslant 3\) and one writes

\[ \operatorname{prof} \operatorname{hop}_Y^{\mathbf{L}}(X)\geqslant 3 \]

if, for every finite constant sheaf of \(\mathbf{L}\)-groups \(F\) on \(X\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant 3\). If \(\mathbf{L}=\mathbf{P}\), one omits \(\mathbf{L}\) in the notation.

Corollary 1.3

Under the conditions of 1.1, if \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant n\), then, for every closed set \(Z\) of \(Y\), one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_Z(F)\geqslant n. \]

Let us carry out the argument, for example, in the case where \(F\) is a complex of abelian sheaves with degrees bounded below. One uses 1.1 \(3^\circ)\) (ii). Let \(V=X-Z\) and consider, for every integer \(p\), the sequence of morphisms

\[ \operatorname{H}^p(X, F)\xrightarrow{f} \operatorname{H}^p(V, F)\xrightarrow{g} \operatorname{H}^p(U, F). \]

By hypothesis \(g\) and \(f\circ g\) are bijective for \(p<n-1\) and injective for \(p=n-1\); the same is therefore true of \(f\). Since the argument is valid when one replaces \(X\) by a scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\), this proves 1.3.

Corollary 1.4

The notation is that of 1.1 \(2^\circ)\). If \(X'\) is a scheme over \(X\), let us denote by \(\Phi'\) the functor which associates to an étale covering of \(X'\) its restriction to \(U'\), and by \(\Phi'_{F'}\) the functor which associates to a torsor2 under \(F'\) its restriction to \(U'\). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) One has \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant 1\) (resp. \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant 2\), resp. \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant 3\)).

(ii) For every scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\), the functor \(\Phi'_{F'}\) is faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. an equivalence of categories).

In particular, in order that one have \(\operatorname{prof}_Y({X})\geqslant 1\) (resp. \(\operatorname{prof}_Y({X})\geqslant 2\), resp. \(\operatorname{prof} \operatorname{hop}_Y(X)\geqslant 3\)), it is necessary and sufficient that the functor \(\Phi'\) be faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. an equivalence of categories).

This follows indeed from 1.1 \(2^\circ)\) (ii), taking into account the interpretation of \(\operatorname{H}^1(X', F')\) as the set of classes (mod. isomorphisms) of torsors under \(F'\) (SGA 4 VII 2), and of the étale coverings \(Z\) of degree \(n\) of a scheme as associated to principal Galois coverings with group the symmetric group \(\mathfrak{S}_n\), with \(Z\) being associated to the covering \(\underline{\mathrm{Isom}}_X(Z_0, Z)\), where \(Z_0\) is the trivial covering of \(X\) of degree \(n\).

Corollary 1.5

Under the conditions of 1.1 \(3^\circ)\), suppose that one has \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant n\); then one has

\[ \operatorname{H}_Y^n(X, F)\simeq \operatorname{H}^0(X, {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^n(F)). \]

The corollary follows from the spectral sequence

\[ \operatorname{E}_2^{pq}=H^p(X, {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^q(F))\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} H_Y^*(X, F). \]

Indeed one has by hypothesis \(\operatorname{E}_2^{pq}=0\) for \(q<n\), whence it follows that

\[ \operatorname{H}_Y^n(X, F)=\operatorname{E}_2^{0n}=\operatorname{H}^0(X, {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^n(F)). \]
Remarks 1.6

a) The notion of \(Y\)-depth, in the form of the equivalent conditions (i) and (ii) of 1.1, makes sense for any site. In the particular case where \(X\) is a locally noetherian scheme, equipped with the Zariski topology, and \(F\) a sheaf of coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-modules, one recovers the usual notion of \(Y\)-depth as the infimum of the depths at the points of \(Y\) (III).

b) For \(n\leqslant 2\), the notion of étale \(Y\)-depth of \(X\) is independent of \(\mathbf{L}\). For \(n=1\), it means simply that \(U\) is dense in \(X\). Indeed this condition is necessary in order that one have \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant 1\), and it is also sufficient because one may suppose \(X\) reduced, a case in which the condition \(U\) dense in \(X\) is preserved under étale base change (EGA IV 11.10.5) (ii) b)). If \(U\) is retrocompact in \(X\), the relation \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(X)\geqslant 1\) is also equivalent to saying that \(Y\) contains no maximal point of \(X\) (EGA I 6.6.5). For \(n=2\) and \(U\) retrocompact in \(X\), the condition \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(X)\geqslant 2\) is equivalent to the fact that, for every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y\), \(\overline{U}\) is connected and non-empty, that is to say that "\(Y\) does not disconnect \(X\), locally for the étale topology".

c) If \(X\) is of \(Y\)-depth \(\geqslant n\) for \(\mathbf{L}\) and \(U\) retrocompact in \(X\), then for every locally constant abelian sheaf of \(\mathbf{L}\)-torsion \(F\) on \(X\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(X)\geqslant n\). Indeed, the property \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant n\) being local for the étale topology, one may suppose \(F\) constant; then \(F\) is a filtered direct limit of sheaves that are finite sums of sheaves of the form \(\mathbf{Z}/p^m\mathbf{Z}\), where \(m\) is an integer \(>0\) and \(p\in \mathbf{L}\). Using 1.1 (iii) and (SGA 4 VII 3.3), one sees that one can reduce to the case where \(F=\mathbf{Z}/p^m\mathbf{Z}\), then, by induction on \(m\), to the case where \(F=\mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z}\) for which the assertion follows from the definition.

d) By 1.4, if one has \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(X)\geqslant 3\), the pair \((X, Y)\) is pure in the sense of X 3.1. In fact the pure pairs that one encounters in practice (cf. X 3.4) satisfy the stronger condition of homotopic depth \(\geqslant 3\), and this notion can therefore advantageously be substituted for that of a pure pair.

e) Let \(F\) be a complex of abelian sheaves and \(T(F)\) the complex obtained by applying to \(F\) the translation functor ([3]); then one obviously has:

\[ \operatorname{prof}_Y(T(F))=\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)-1. \]

f) Let us point out that the recent work of Artin-Mazur ([1]) makes it possible to define the notion of homotopic depth \(\geqslant n\), whatever the integer \(n\) (not only if \(n\geqslant 3\)).

g) Under the conditions of 1.1 3), in order that one have \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)=\infty\), it is necessary and sufficient that one have \(F\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} Ri_*(i^*F)\) in \(D^+(X)\). Indeed, the \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F)\) are the cohomology sheaves of the cone (= mapping cylinder) of the canonical morphism \(F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Ri_*i^*(F)\).

Definition 1.7

Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(x\) a point of \(X\), \(\overline{x}\) a geometric point above \(x\) and \(\overline{X}\) the strict localization of \(X\) at \(\overline{x}\). As previously, \(F\) denotes either a sheaf of sets on \(X\), or a sheaf of groups on \(X\), or a complex of abelian sheaves on \(X\) with degrees bounded below, \(\overline{F}\) its inverse image on \(\overline{X}\) and \(\mathbf{L}\) a set of prime numbers. One says that \(F\) is of étale depth \(\geqslant n\) at the point \(x\) (resp. that the étale depth for \(\mathbf{L}\) of \(X\) at \(x\) is \(\geqslant n\), resp. that the homotopic depth for \(\mathbf{L}\) of \(X\) at \(x\) is \(\geqslant 3\)) and one writes \(\operatorname{prof}_x(F)\geqslant n\) (resp. \(\operatorname{prof}_x^{\mathbf{L}}(X)\geqslant n\), resp. \(\operatorname{prof} \operatorname{hop}_x^{\mathbf{L}}(X)\geqslant 3\)) if one has \(\operatorname{prof}_{\overline{x}}(\overline{F})>n\) (resp. \(\operatorname{prof}_{\overline{x}}^{\mathbf{L}}(\overline{X})> n\), resp. \(\operatorname{prof} \operatorname{hop}_{\overline{x}}^{\mathbf{L}}(\overline{X})\geqslant 3\)). One defines in the obvious way the integer \(\operatorname{prof}_x^{\mathbf{L}}(X)\) and, if \(F\) is a complex of abelian sheaves, the integer \(\operatorname{prof}_x(F)\). If \(\mathbf{L}\) is the set of all prime numbers, one omits \(\mathbf{L}\) in the notation \(\operatorname{prof}_x^{\mathbf{L}}(X)\), except if there is a risk of confusion with the notation of (EGA IV 5.7.1), in which case one writes \(\operatorname{prof} {\textup{ét}}_x(X)\).

One then has the following pointwise characterization of depth:

Theorem 1.8

Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\) such that the open set \(U=X-Y\) is retrocompact in \(X\). If \(F\) is either a sheaf of sets on \(X\), or a sheaf of groups on \(X\), or a complex of abelian sheaves on \(X\) with degrees bounded below, then one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_Y(F)=\inf_{y\in Y} \operatorname{prof}_y(F). \]

1.8.1

Let us first show that, for every point \(y\) of \(Y\), one has the inequality \(\operatorname{prof}_y(F)\geqslant \operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\). Indeed, let \(\overline{y}\) be a geometric point above \(y\), \(\overline{X}\) the strict localization of \(X\) at \(\overline{y}\), \(\overline{F}\) and \(\overline{Y}\) the inverse images of \(F\) and \(Y\) on \(\overline{X}\). One has, by 1.7 and 1.3

\[ \operatorname{prof}_y(F)=\operatorname{prof}_{\overline{y}}(\overline{F})\geqslant \operatorname{prof}_{\overline{Y}}(\overline{F})\geqslant \operatorname{prof}_Y(F), \]

the last inequality using the hypothesis "\(U\) retrocompact", via conditions (iii) in 1.1 and the transitivity in the formation of strict localizations.

1.8.2

Conversely, suppose that, for every point \(y\) of \(Y\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}_y(Y)\geqslant n\) (\(n\) an integer) and let us show that one then has \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant n\).

Let us first recall the following well-known results (SGA 4 VIII):

Lemma 1.8.2.1

Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(F\) a sheaf of sets on \(X\) (resp. \(G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F\) a monomorphism of sheaves of sets on \(X\)). Then, in order that two sections \(s\) and \(s'\) of \(F\) coincide (resp. in order that a section \(s\) of \(F\) come from a section of \(G\)), it is necessary and sufficient that this be so locally. In particular, if \(s\) and \(s'\) are two sections of \(F\), there exists a largest open set \(V\) of \(X\) on which they coincide (resp. if \(s\) is a section of \(F\) on \(X\), there exists a largest open set \(V\) of \(X\) such that \(s|V\) comes from a section of \(G\) on \(V\)). This open set is also the set of points \(x\) of \(X\) such that, denoting by \(\overline{x}\) a geometric point above \(x\), the sections \(s\) and \(s'\) have the same image in the stalk \(F_{\overline{x}}\) (resp. that the image of \(s\) in \(F_{\overline{x}}\) comes from an element of \(G_{\overline{x}}\)).

Let us return to the proof of 1.8.

\(1^\circ)\) Case where \(F\) is a sheaf of sets. If \(n=1\), it suffices to show that the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(U, F) \]

is injective, the result still applying when one replaces \(X\) by a scheme étale over \(X\). Let \(s\) and \(s'\) be two sections of \(F\) over \(X\) which have the same image in \(\operatorname{H}^0(U, F)\) and let \(V\) be the largest open set over which they are equal; one obviously has \(V\supset U\). Suppose \(V\neq X\) and let \(y\) be a maximal point of \(X-V\), \(\overline{y}\) a geometric point above \(y\), \(\overline{X}\) the strict localization of \(X\) at \(\overline{y}\) and \(\overline{V}\) and \(\overline{F}\) the inverse images of \(V\) and \(F\) on \(\overline{X}\). By the choice of \(y\), one has \(\overline{X}-\overline{y}=\overline{V}\) and by hypothesis, the morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(\overline{X}, \overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(\overline{X}-\overline{y}, \overline{F})=\operatorname{H}^0(\overline{V}, \overline{F}) \]

is injective. It follows that \(s\) and \(s'\) coincide at the point \(y\), which is absurd. If \(n=2\), it suffices to show, taking into account what precedes, that the morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(U, F)=\operatorname{H}^0(X, i_*i^*F) \]

is surjective (where \(i\) is the canonical immersion of \(U\) in \(X\)). Let \(s\) be a section of \(i_*i^*F\) over \(X\) and \(V\) the largest open set over which it comes from a section of \(F\). Suppose \(V\neq X\) and let \(y\) be a maximal point of \(X-V\); with the preceding notation, it follows from the hypothesis that the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(\overline{X}, \overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(\overline{X}-\overline{y}, \overline{F})=\operatorname{H}^0(\overline{V}, \overline{F}) \]

is bijective; consequently \(s|\overline{V}\) extends to the point \(\overline{y}\), which is absurd and completes the proof in case \(1^\circ)\).

\(2^\circ)\) Case where \(F\) is a sheaf of groups. Taking into account \(1^\circ)\), the only thing that remains to be shown is that, in the case \(n=3\), the morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^1(X, F)=\operatorname{H}^1(X, i_*i^*F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^1(U, F) \]

is bijective. We already know that it is injective thanks to \(1^\circ)\) and to 1.1 \(2^\circ)\) (ii bis). For surjectivity, one uses the exact sequence (SGA 4 XII 3.2)

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^1(X, i_*i^*F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^1(U, F)\xrightarrow{d}\operatorname{H}^0(X, \operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F)). \]

Let \(s\in \operatorname{H}^1(U, F)\) and \(V\supset U\) the largest open set over which \(d(s)=0\); it is also the largest open set such that \(s\) comes from an element of \(\operatorname{H}^1(V, F)\). Suppose \(V\neq X\) and let \(y\) be a maximal point of \(X-V\); if \(\overline{X}\) is the strict localization of \(X\) at a geometric point \(\overline{y}\) above \(y\), one has, with obvious notation, the exact sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^1(\overline{X}, \overline{i}_*(\overline{i}^*\overline{F}))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^1(\overline{U}, \overline{F})\xrightarrow{\overline{d}}\operatorname{H}^0(\overline{X}, \operatorname{R}^1\overline{i}_*(\overline{i}^*\overline{F})). \]

Since \(i:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is quasi-compact, \(\operatorname{R}^1\overline{i}_*(\overline{i}^*\overline{F})\) is the inverse image of \(\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F)\) under the morphism \(\overline{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), whence \(\operatorname{H}^0(\overline{X}, \operatorname{R}^1\overline{i}_*(\overline{i}^*\overline{F}))=(\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F))_{\overline{y}}\). By hypothesis and since \(y\in Y\), the morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^1(\overline{X}, \overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^1(\overline{V}, \overline{F}) \]

is bijective. The image \(\overline{s}\) of \(s\) in \(\operatorname{H}^1(\overline{U}, \overline{F})\), which extends to \(\overline{V}\) by definition of \(V\), therefore also extends to \(\overline{X}\); it follows that \(\overline{d}(\overline{s})=0\) hence the image of \(d(s)\) in the geometric stalk \((\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F))_{\overline{y}}\) is zero; but this contradicts the definition of \(V\), whence case \(2^\circ)\).

\(3^\circ)\) Case where \(F\) is a complex of abelian sheaves, with degrees bounded below. One argues by induction on \(n\). The conclusion is satisfied for \(n\) small enough, since \(F\) has degrees bounded below. Suppose then that \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant n-1\) and let us show that \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant n\), knowing that, for every point \(y\) of \(Y\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}_y(F)\geqslant n\). It suffices to see that the canonical morphism

\[ \tag{*} {\operatorname{H}^{n-2}(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^{n-2}(U, F)} \]

is surjective and that

\[ \tag{**} {\operatorname{H}^{n-1}(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^{n-1}(U, F)} \]

is injective (the result applying when one replaces \(X\) by a scheme étale over \(X\)).

a) Surjectivity of (*). The proof is analogous to that of \(2^\circ)\). Taking into account 1.5 and (SGA 4 V 4.5), one has the exact sequence

\[ \operatorname{H}^{n-2}(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^{n-2}(U, F)\xrightarrow{d}\operatorname{H}^{n-1}_Y(X, F)=\operatorname{H}^0(X, {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^{n-1}(F)). \]

Let \(s\in \operatorname{H}^{n-2}(U, F)\) and \(V\supset U\) the largest open set over which \(d(s)=0\), which is also the largest open set such that \(s\) extends to \(\operatorname{H}^{n-2}(V, F)\). Suppose \(V\neq X\) and let \(y\) be a maximal point of \(X-V\) and \(\overline{X}\) the strict localization of \(X\) at a geometric point \(\overline{y}\) above \(y\). Since \(i:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is quasi-compact, the formation of \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^{n-1}(F)\) commutes with the base change \(\overline{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) and one therefore has (with obvious notation) the exact sequence

\[ \operatorname{H}^{n-2}(\overline{X}, \overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^{n-2}(\overline{U}, \overline{F})\xrightarrow{\overline{d}}\operatorname{H}^{n-1}_{\overline{Y}}(\overline{X}, \overline{F})=({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^{n-1}(F))_{\overline{y}}, \]

the last equality following from the retrocompactness hypothesis on \(U\).

Now one has by hypothesis the isomorphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^{n-2}(\overline{X}, \overline{F})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{H}^{n-2}(\overline{X}-\overline{y}, \overline{F})=\operatorname{H}^{n-2}(\overline{V}, \overline{F}); \]

consequently the image \(\overline{s}\) of \(s\) in \(\operatorname{H}^{n-2}(\overline{U}, \overline{F})\), which extends (by definition of \(V\)) to \(\operatorname{H}^{n-2}(\overline{V}, \overline{F})\), also extends to \(\operatorname{H}^{n-2}(\overline{X}, \overline{F})\); but this shows that \(\overline{d}(\overline{s})=0\), that is to say that \(d(s)\) is zero at \(\overline{y}\), which is absurd.

b) Injectivity of (**). Using the surjectivity of (*), one obtains the exact sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(X, {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^{n-1}(F))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^{n-1}(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^{n-1}(U, F) \]

and one must show that every element \(s\in \operatorname{H}^0(X, {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^{n-1}(F))\) is zero. Let \(V\) be the largest open set over which \(s=0\). Suppose that one has \(V\neq X\) and let \(y\) be a maximal point of \(X-V\), \(\overline{X}\) a strict localization of \(X\) at a geometric point \(\overline{y}\) above \(y\). One has, by the induction hypothesis and by 1.8.1, the relation \(\operatorname{prof}_{\overline{Y}}(\overline{F})\geqslant \operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant n-1\), whence the fact that the map \(e\) of the following diagram is injective:

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(\overline{X}, {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^{n-1}(\overline{F}))= ({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^{n-1}(F))_{\overline{y}}\xrightarrow{e} \operatorname{H}^{n-1}(\overline{X}, \overline{F}) \xrightarrow{f} \operatorname{H}^{n-1}(\overline{V}, \overline{F}). \]

The same is true of \(f\) by virtue of the hypothesis; the equality on the left follows from the retrocompactness hypothesis on \(U\). Let \(\overline{s}\) be the image of \(s\) in \(({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^{n-1}(F))_{\overline{y}}\); since \(s\) vanishes over \(V\), one has \(f\cdot e(\overline{s})=0\), whence \(\overline{s}=0\), which contradicts the choice of \(y\) and completes the proof.

Remark 1.9

A result analogous to 1.8 is no doubt valid in the case where one replaces the étale topos of a scheme \(X\) by a "topos locally of finite type", that is to say one definable by a site locally of finite type (SGA 4 VI 1.1). To see this, one must use a result of P. Deligne (SGA 4 XVII), asserting that there are "enough fibre functors" in such a topos.

We shall deduce from 1.8 some important cases where one can determine the étale depth.

Theorem 1.10

[Cohomological semi-purity theorem] Let us denote by \(X\) either a smooth scheme over a field \(k\), or an excellent regular scheme (EGA IV 7.8.2) of characteristic zero (N.B. if one admits resolution of singularities in the sense of (SGA 4 XIX), it suffices to suppose, more generally, that \(X\) is an excellent regular scheme of equal characteristic). Let \(Y\) be a closed subset of \(X\) and \(\mathbf{L}\) the set of prime numbers distinct from the characteristic of \(X\). Then one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_Y^{\mathbf{L}}(X)=2\operatorname{codim}(Y, X). \]
Proof▾

It follows from 1.8 that one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_Y^{\mathbf{L}}(X)=\inf_{y\in Y} \operatorname{prof}_y(X). \]

Since on the other hand \(\operatorname{codim}(Y, X)=\inf_{y\in Y} \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, y}\), one is reduced to showing that

\[ \operatorname{prof}_y^{\mathbf{L}}(X)=2 \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, y}, \]

which follows from (SGA 4 XVI 3.7 and XIX 3.2).

Q.E.D.
Theorem 1.11 (Homotopic purity theorem)

If \(X\) is a locally noetherian scheme which is regular (resp. whose local rings are complete intersections), \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\) such that \(\operatorname{codim}(Y, X)\geqslant 2\) (resp. \(\operatorname{codim}(Y, X)\geqslant3\)), then one has

\[ \operatorname{prof} \operatorname{hop}_Y(X)\geqslant 3. \]

It follows indeed from 1.8 that one has \(\operatorname{prof} \operatorname{hop}_Y(X)=\inf_{y\in Y} \operatorname{prof} \operatorname{hop}_y(X)\). Now the strictly local rings of \(X\) at the various points of \(Y\) are regular rings of dimension \(\geqslant 2\) (resp. complete intersection rings of dimension \(\geqslant 3\)). It then follows from the purity theorem X 3.4 that \(\operatorname{prof} \operatorname{hop}_y(X)\geqslant 3\), which proves the theorem.

Example 1.12

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian scheme, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\) and \(n=1\) or \(2\). Then, if one has \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(\mathscr{O}_X)\geqslant n\) (\(\operatorname{prof}_Y(\mathscr{O}_X)\) denoting the \(Y\)-depth in the sense of coherent sheaves (cf. 1.6 a)), one also has \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(X)\geqslant n\); this is obvious for \(n=1\) and, for \(n=2\), it is nothing other than Hartshorne's theorem (III 1). On the other hand the analogous assertion is false for \(n\geqslant 3\). Take for example an affine space of dimension \(\geqslant 3\) over a field of characteristic \(\neq 2\) and let the group \(\mathbf{Z}/2\mathbf{Z}\) act by symmetry with respect to the origin. Let \(X\) be the quotient and \(Y=\{x\}\) the image of the origin in \(X\). Then \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) is a Cohen-Macaulay ring, hence one has \(\operatorname{prof}_x(\mathscr{O}_X)\geqslant 3\); but the affine space minus the origin is an étale covering of \(X-\{x\}\) which does not extend to an étale covering of \(X\); hence one has by 1.4 \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(X)=2\).

The following theorem is the analogue of (EGA IV 6.3.1):

Theorem 1.13

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\), \(Z\) a closed subset of \(S\), such that \(f(Y)\subset Z\). One supposes that the local rings of \(X\) at the various points of \(Y\) are noetherian and that the open sets \(X-Y\) and \(S-Z\) are retrocompact in \(X\) and \(S\) respectively. Let \(p\), \(q\), \(r\) be integers such that \(p\geqslant -r\), \(q\geqslant 0\), \(\mathbf{L}\) a set of prime numbers and \(F\) a complex of abelian sheaves of \(\mathbf{L}\)-torsion on \(S\), such that the cohomology sheaves \(\operatorname{H}^i(F)\) are zero for \(i<-r\). One supposes that

  1. The morphism \(f\) is locally (\(p+q+r-2\))-acyclic for \(\mathbf{L}\) (SGA 4 XV 1.11).

  2. One has

    \[ \operatorname{prof}_Z(F)\geqslant p. \]

  3. For every point \(s\) of \(Z\), one has

    \[ \operatorname{prof}_{Y_s}^{\mathbf{L}}(X_s)\geqslant q. \]

Then one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_Y(f^*F)\geqslant p+q. \]

We shall need the following lemma:

Lemma 1.13.1

Let \(\mathbf{L}\) be a set of prime numbers, \(n\) and \(r\) integers, \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism locally \(n\)-acyclic for \(\mathbf{L}\). Let \(F\) be a complex of abelian sheaves, with cohomology sheaves of \(\mathbf{L}\)-torsion, such that \(\operatorname{H}^i(F)=0\) for \(i<-r\), \(Z\) a closed subset of \(S\) such that \(S-Z\) is retrocompact in \(S\) and \(T=f^{-1}(Z)\). Then the canonical morphism

\[ f^*({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Z^i(F))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_T^i(f^*F) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-r+2\) and injective for \(i=n-r+2\).

Set \(U=S-Z\) and \(V=X-T\), so that one has the cartesian square

\[ \xymatrix{ V \ar[r]^-g\ar[d]_k& U\ar[d]^j\\ X \ar[r]^-f&S } \]

Consider the following commutative diagram whose rows are exact

\[ \xymatrix{ {}\ar[r]&{f^*(\underline{\mathrm{H}}_Z^i(F))}\ar[r]\ar[d]& {f^*(\underline{\mathrm{H}}^i(F))}\ar[r]\ar[d]^{\wr} &{f^*(\underline{\mathrm{H}}^i(\mathrm{R} j_*(j^*F)))}\ar[r]\ar[d]& \\ {}\ar[r] &{\underline{\mathrm{H}}_T^i(f^*F)}\ar[r]& {\underline{\mathrm{H}}^i(f^*F)} \ar[r]&{\underline{\mathrm{H}}^i(\mathrm{R} k_*(k^*f^*F))} \ar[r]&\text{;}} \]

it follows that one is reduced to showing that the morphism

\[ f^*({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}^i(\operatorname{R} j_*(j^*F)))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}^i(\operatorname{R} k_*(k^*f^*F)) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-r+1\) and injective for \(i=n-r+1\). Now such a morphism comes from the following morphism between hypercohomology spectral sequences

\[ \xymatrix{{f^*E_2^{p, q}=f^*(\mathrm{R}^pj_*(\underline{\mathrm{H}}^q(j^*F)))}\ar@{=>} [r]\ar[d]&{f^*(\underline{\mathrm{H}}^*(\mathrm{R} j_*(j^*F)))}\ar[d]\\ {E^{\prime p, q}_2=\mathrm{R}^pk_*(\underline{\mathrm{H}}^q(k^*f^*F))}\ar@{=>}[r]&{\underline{\mathrm{H}}^*(\mathrm{R} k_*(k^*f^*F)).}} \]

Since \(j\) is quasi-compact, it follows from (SGA 4 XV 1.10) that the morphism \(f^*(E^{p, q}_2)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E'^{p, q}_2\) is bijective for \(p\leqslant n\) and injective for \(p=n+1\); in particular it is bijective for \(p+q\leqslant n-r\) and injective for \(p+q=n-r+1\). The conclusion follows at once.

Let us return to the proof of 1.13. Let \(T=f^{-1}(Z)\). By 1.13.1 and condition a), the canonical morphism \(f^*(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Z^i(F))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_T^i(f^*F)\) is an isomorphism for \(i\leqslant p+q\). It therefore follows from b) that \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^i_T(f^*F)=0\) for \(i<p\) and, for \(i<p+q, \ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^i_T(f^*F)\) restricted to \(T\) is the inverse image of a sheaf \(G^i\) on \(Z\). Let

\[ f_T:T\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z \]

be the restriction of \(f\) to \(T\). It then follows from c) and from the corollary that follows that \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^j_Y(f^*_T(G^i))=0\) for \(j<q\). One concludes that

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^j_Y(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^i_T(f^*F))=0\text{ for } i+j<p+q, \]

since the inequality \(i+j<p+q\) implies either \(i<p\) and then \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^i_T(f^*F)=0\), or \(j<q\) and then \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^j(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_T^i(f^*F))=0\). Given that one has, with the notation of 1.0, \(\underline{\Gamma}_Y=\underline{\Gamma}_Y.\underline{\Gamma}_T\), one has the spectral sequence

\[ \tag{1.13.2} E_2^{i, j}=\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^j(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_T^i(f^*F))\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^*_Y(f^*F); \]

since \(E_2^{i, j}=0\) for \(i+j<p+q\), one sees that \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^k(f^*F)=0\) for \(k<p+q\). The theorem will therefore be proved if one proves the following corollary (which is the particular case of 1.13 obtained by setting \(Z=S, \ r=p=0\) and \(F\) reduced to degree \(0\)).

Corollary 1.14

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism, \(Y\) a closed subset such that the complementary open set \(X- Y\) is retrocompact in \(X\) and such that the local rings of \(X\) at the various points of \(Y\) are noetherian. Let \(\mathbf{L}\) be a set of prime numbers, \(q\) an integer and \(F\) an abelian sheaf of \(\mathbf{L}\)-torsion on \(S\). Suppose that \(f\) is locally \((q-2)\)-acyclic for \(\mathbf{L}\) and that, for every point \(s\) of \(S\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}_{Y_s}^\mathbf{L}(X_s)\!\geqslant\! q\). Then, one has \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(f^*F)\geqslant q\).

\(1^\circ)\) Reduction to the case where \(X\) and \(S\) are strictly local schemes, \(f\) a local morphism and \(Y\) reduced to a closed point of \(X\).

By 1.8, to establish 1.14, one must show that one has for every point \(y\) of \(Y\):

\[ \operatorname{prof}_y(f^*F)\geqslant q. \]

Let \(s=f(y)\), \(\overline{s}\) a geometric point above \(s\), \(\overline{y}\) a geometric point above \(y\) and above \(\overline{s}\), \(\overline{X}\) and \(\overline{S}\) the strict localizations of \(X\) and \(S\) at \(\overline{y}\) and \(\overline{s}\) respectively, \(\overline{f}:\overline{X}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\overline{S}\) the canonical morphism and \(\overline{F}\) the inverse image of \(F\) on \(\overline{S}\). Since one has the relation \(\operatorname{prof}_y(f^*F)=\operatorname{prof}_{\overline{y}}(\overline{f}^*\overline{F})\), it suffices to show that the hypotheses of 1.14 are preserved when one replaces \(f\) (resp. \(Y\), resp. \(F\)) by \(\overline{f}\) (resp. \(\{\overline{y}\}\), resp. \(\overline{F}\)). The retrocompactness condition follows from the noetherian hypothesis on \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\), implying that \(\overline{X}\) is noetherian. By (SGA 4 XV 1.10 (i)), \(\overline{f}\) is still locally \((q-2)\)-acyclic for \(\mathbf{L}\). On the other hand the fibre \((\overline{X})_{\overline{s}}\) of \(\overline{X}\) above \(\overline{s}\) is identified with the strict localization of \(X_s\) at \(\overline{y}\), hence satisfies the relation \(\operatorname{prof}_{\overline{y}}((\overline{X})_{\overline{s}})\geqslant q\). Since an analogous relation is trivially verified for the fibres of the \(\overline{S}\)-scheme \(\overline{X}\) other than the closed fibre, this completes the reduction.

\(2^\circ)\) Case where \(X\) and \(S\) are strictly local, \(f\) a local homomorphism and \(Y\) reduced to the closed point of \(X\). Let

\[ g:Y=X-\{y\}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S \]

be the structural morphism of \(U\). One must show that the canonical morphism

\[ u_i:H^i(X, f^*F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i(U, f^*F) \]

is bijective for \(i\leqslant q-2\) and injective for \(i=q-1\). Consider the commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{H^i(X, f^*F)\ar[rr]^{u_i}&& H^i(U, f^*F)\\&H^i(S, F)\ar[lu]^{v_i}\ar[ru]_{w_i}} \]

The morphism \(v_i\) is obviously bijective for every \(i\). On the other hand \(g\) is locally \((q-2)\)-acyclic for \(\mathbf{L}\); moreover its fibres are \((q-2)\)-acyclic for \(\mathbf{L}\), as follows from the fact that \(\operatorname{prof}_y(X_s)\geqslant q\) and that the fibres of \(f\) are \((q-2)\)-acyclic for \(\mathbf{L}\); since \(g\) is quasi-compact because \(X\) is noetherian, it follows from (SGA 4 XV 1.16) that \(g\) is \((q-2)\)-acyclic for \(\mathbf{L}\). Consequently \(w_i\), hence also \(u_i\), is bijective for \(i\leqslant q-2\) and injective for \(i=q-1\), which completes the proof of 1.14.

Corollary 1.15

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathbf{L}\) a set of prime numbers, \(m\) and \(r\) integers and \(F\) a complex of abelian sheaves of \(\mathbf{L}\)-torsion on \(S\) such that \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^i(F)=0\) for \(i<-r\). Let \(x\) be a point of \(X,\ s=f(x)\) and suppose that the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) is noetherian. Then, if \(f\) is locally \(m\)-acyclic for \(\mathbf{L}\), one has the relation

\[ \tag{*} {\operatorname{prof}_x(f^*F)\geqslant\inf(\operatorname{prof}_s(F)+\operatorname{prof}^\mathbf{L}_xX_s), n)\text{ where }n=m-r+2.} \]

In particular, if \(n\geqslant\operatorname{prof}_s(F)+\operatorname{prof}_x^\mathbf{L}(X_s)\), for example if \(f\) is locally acyclic for \(\mathbf{L}\), one has

\[ \tag{**} {\operatorname{prof}_x(f^*F)\geqslant\operatorname{prof}_s(F)+\operatorname{prof}_x^\mathbf{L}(X_s).} \]

If \(\mathbf{L}\) is reduced to a single element \(\ell\) and if one has \(n\geqslant \operatorname{prof}_s(F)+\operatorname{prof}_x^\mathbf{L}(X_s)\), the preceding inequality is an equality.

One reduces to the case where \(s\) and \(x\) are closed points, by taking the strict localizations of \(S\) and \(x\) at geometric points \(\overline{s}\) above \(s\) and \(\overline{x}\) above \(x\) and above \(\overline{s}\). If one has the inequality \(n\geqslant\operatorname{prof}_s(F)+\operatorname{prof}_x^\mathbf{L}(X_s)\), then (\(*\)) is obtained from 1.13 by setting \(p=\operatorname{prof}_s(F)\) and \(q=\operatorname{prof}_x^\mathbf{L}(X_s)\) (the hypothesis that \(S-\{s\}\) is retrocompact in \(S\) follows from the fact that \(X-X_s\) is retrocompact in \(X\) and that \(f\) is surjective since it is \((-1)\)-acyclic (except perhaps if the conclusion of 1.15 is empty)). If one has \(n<\operatorname{prof}_s(F)+\operatorname{prof}_x^\mathbf{L}(X_s)\), the inequality (*) is again obtained from 1.13 by setting for example \(p=\operatorname{prof}_s(F)\) and \(q=n-p\). It remains to prove the last assertion. Let \(p=\operatorname{prof}_s(F)\) and \(q=\operatorname{prof}_x(X_s)\); it follows from (1.13.2) that one has

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_x^{p+q}(f^*(F))\simeq \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_x^q(f^*(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^p_s(F))). \]

Since \(\operatorname{prof}_s(F)=p\), the sheaf \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^p_s(F)\) is a sheaf of \(\ell\)-torsion, constant on \(s\), different from zero. Consequently the sheaf \(G=f^*(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_s^p(F))\) is a sheaf of \(\ell\)-torsion, constant on \(X_s\), non-zero, hence contains a subsheaf isomorphic to \(\mathbf{Z}/\ell\mathbf{Z}\); since \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_x^q(\mathbf{Z}/\ell\mathbf{Z})\) is different from zero, one indeed has \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_x^q(G)\neq 0\).

Corollary 1.16

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a regular morphism of excellent schemes (EGA IV 7.8.2) of characteristic zero, \(\ell\) a prime number and \(F\) a complex of sheaves of \(\ell\)-torsion on \(S\). Let \(x\in X\), \(s=f(x)\); then one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_x(f^*F)=\operatorname{prof}_s(F)+2\dim(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}). \]

Indeed \(f\) is regular by definition of "excellent" hence locally acyclic (SGA 4 XIX 4.1). It then follows from 1.15 that one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_x(f^*F)=\operatorname{prof}_s(F)+\operatorname{prof}_x(X_s). \]

Now one has by 1.10

\[ \operatorname{prof}_x(X_s)=2\dim\mathscr{O}_{X_s, x}, \]

whence the result.

Remark 1.17

It follows from 1.15 that 1.13 remains true when one replaces b) and c) by the conditions:

b\('\)) For every point \(s\in f(Y)\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}_s(F)\geqslant p\).

c\('\)) For every point \(x\in Y\), if \(s=f(x)\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}^\mathbf{L}_x(X_s)\geqslant q\).

In the case of a sheaf of sets or of groups, one has the following theorem analogous to 1.13.

Theorem 1.18

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\) such that \(X-Y\) is retrocompact in \(X\) and such that, for every point \(x\) of \(Y\), the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) is noetherian.

\(1^\circ)\) Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets on \(S\) and \(n\) an integer equal to \(1\) or \(2\). Suppose that \(f\) is locally \((n-2)\)-acyclic and that, for every point \(s\) of \(f(Y)\), one has:

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{Y_s}(X_s)+\operatorname{prof}_s(F)\geqslant n. \]

Then one has:

\[ \operatorname{prof}_Y(f^*F)\geqslant n. \]

\(2^\circ)\) Let \(\mathbf{L}\) be a set of prime numbers and \(F\) a sheaf of ind-\(\mathbf{L}\)-groups. Suppose that \(f\) is locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbf{L}\) (SGA 4 XV 1.11) and that, for every point \(s\) of \(f(Y)\), one has:

\[ \operatorname{prof}\operatorname{hop}^\mathbf{L}_{Y_s}(X_s)+\operatorname{prof}_s(F)\geqslant 3. \]

Then, one has:

\[ \operatorname{prof}_Y(f^*F)\geqslant 3. \]
Proof▾

One reduces, as in 1.14 and 1.15, to the case where \(X\) and \(S\) are strictly local schemes, \(f\) a local homomorphism and \(Y\) the closed point \(x\) of \(X\). Let \(s=f(x)\) be the closed point of \(S\); one has the commutative diagram:

\[ \xymatrix{ X-X_s\ar[r]^i\ar[d]_h&X-\{x\}\ar[rd]_g\ar[rr]^j&&X\ar[ld]^f\\ S-\{s\}\ar[rr]_k&&S. } \]

\(1^\circ)\) a) Case \(n=1\).

If one has \(\operatorname{prof}_s(F)\geqslant 1\), then the morphism \(F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k_*k^*F\) is injective, hence the morphism \(f^*F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f^*(k_*k^*F)\) is also injective. On the other hand it follows from the fact that \(f\) is locally \((-1)\)-acyclic, that the morphism \(f^*(k_*k^*F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (j.i)*(f^*F{|X-X_s}))\) is injective. Finally, the composite morphism \(f^*F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (j.i)*(f^*F{|X-X_s}))\) is injective, which shows that one has \(\operatorname{prof}_{X_s}(f^*F)\geqslant 1\), hence also \(\operatorname{prof}_x(f^*F)\geqslant 1\).

If one has \(\operatorname{prof}_x(X_s)\geqslant 1\), one considers the commutative diagram

\[ \tag{*} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{ H^0(X, f^*F)\ar[r]^-{v}\ar[d]_\wr&H^0(X-\{x\}, f^*F)\ar[d]\\ H^0(X_s, f^*F)\ar[r]^-{v'}&H^0(X-\{x\}, f^*F); } \end{array} \]

By hypothesis, \(v'\) is injective hence the same is true of \(v\).

b) Case \(n=2\). One considers the commutative diagram

\[ \tag{**} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{ H^0(S, F)\ar[d]_{m}^{\wr}\ar[rr]^u&&H^0(S-\{s\}, F)\ar[d]^{\wr}_{ n}\\ H^0(X, f^*F)\ar[r]^-v&H^0(X-\{x\}, f^*F)\ar[r]^w&H^0(X-X_s, f^*F); } \end{array} \]

one must show that \(v\) is bijective. The morphism \(m\) is obviously bijective, and, since \(f\) is \(0\)-acyclic, \(n\) is also bijective.

If one has \(\operatorname{prof}_s(F)\geqslant 2\), \(u\) is bijective. As one has seen in a), the single hypothesis \(\operatorname{prof}_s(F)\geqslant 1\) entails the relation \(\operatorname{prof}_{X_s}(f^*F)\geqslant 1\); consequently \(v\) and \(w\) are injective; it then follows from (**) that \(v\) is bijective.

If one has \(\operatorname{prof}_x(X_s)\geqslant 2\), then \(g\) is \(0\)-acyclic (because it is locally \(0\)-acyclic and its fibres are \(0\)-acyclic). It follows that \(v\cdot m\) is bijective, hence \(v\) is bijective.

If one has \(\operatorname{prof}_s(F)\geqslant 1\) and \(\operatorname{prof}_x(X_s)\geqslant 1\), then one already knows that \(v\) and \(w\) are injective. Let \(z\) be a maximal point of \(X_s-\{x\}\) (such a point exists by the hypothesis \(\operatorname{prof}_x(X_s)\geqslant 1)\), \(\overline{Z}\) the strict localization of \(X\) at a geometric point above \(z\) and \(\overline{f^*F}\) the inverse image of \(f^*F\) on \(\overline{Z}\). Consider the commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=0mm{ &H^0(S, F)\ar[rr]\ar[ld]_{m}^{\sim}&&H^0(S-\{s\}, F)\ar[rd]_{\sim}^{n}\\ H^0(X, f^*F)\ar[rr]^-{v}\ar[rd]_{m'}^{\sim}&&H^0(X-\{x\}, f^*F)\ar[rr]^-{w}\ar[ld]^r&& H^0(X-X_s, f^*F)\ar[ld]^{n'}_{\sim}\\ &H^0(\overline{Z}, f^*F)\ar[rr]&&H^0(\overline{Z}-\{\overline{z}\}, \overline{f^*F})} \]

the morphism \(m'\cdot m\) is obviously bijective and it follows from the fact that \(f\) is locally \(0\)-acyclic that \(n'\cdot n\) is bijective; consequently \(m'\) and \(n'\) are also bijective. Since \(w\) is injective, \(r\) is also injective and consequently \(v\) is bijective.

\(2^\circ)\) Taking b) into account, one already knows that \(\operatorname{prof}_x(f^*F)\geqslant 2\).

If one has \(\operatorname{prof}_s(F)\geqslant 3\), then \(\operatorname{R}^1k_*(k^*F)=1\). Since \(f\) is locally \(1\)-aspherical, one has \(\operatorname{R}^1(j\cdot i)_*(f^*F{|X-X_s})\!=\!f^*(\operatorname{R}^1k_*(k^*F))\!=\!1\). One therefore has \(\operatorname{prof}_{X_s}(f^*F)\!\geqslant\! 3\) and consequently one has \(\operatorname{prof}_x(f^*F)\geqslant 3\).

If one has \(\operatorname{prof}_x(X_s)\geqslant 3\), then \(g\) is \(1\)-aspherical (because \(g\) is locally \(1\)-aspherical and its fibres are \(1\)-aspherical). One therefore has \(H^1(X-\{x\}, f^*F)=H^1(S, F)=1\) and consequently \(\operatorname{prof}_x(f^*F)\geqslant 3\).

If one has \(\operatorname{prof}_s(F)\geqslant 2\) and \(\operatorname{prof}_x(X_s)\geqslant 1\), one uses the exact sequence (SGA 4 XII 3.2):

\[ 1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{R}^1j_*(i_*(f^*F{|X-X_s}))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{R}^1(j.i)_*(f^*F{|X-X_s}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} j_*(\operatorname{R}^1i_*(f*F{|X-X_s})). \]

Since \(f\) and \(g\) are locally \(1\)-aspherical, one has

\begin{align*} \operatorname{R}^1(j\cdot i)_*(f^*F{|X-X_s})&\simeq f^*(\operatorname{R}^1k_*(k^*F)) \\ \operatorname{R}^1i_*(f^*F{|X-X_s})&\simeq g^*(\operatorname{R}^1k_*(k^*F)); \end{align*}

the preceding exact sequence is then written in the form

\[ \tag{{*}{*}{*}} 1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{R}^1j_*(i_*(f^*F{|X-X_s}))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f^*(\operatorname{R}^1k_*(k^*F))\xrightarrow{a} j_*(j^*(f^*(\operatorname{R}^1k_*(k^*F))). \]

The hypothesis \(\operatorname{prof}_s(F)\geqslant 2\) shows that the morphism \(F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k_*k^*F\) is bijective; applying \(g^*\), one finds, taking into account the fact that \(g\) is locally \(0\)-acyclic, \(f^*F{|X-\{x\}}=i_*(f^*F{|X-X_s})\). The hypothesis \(\operatorname{prof}_x(X_s)\geqslant 1\) shows that the morphism \(a\) is injective (note that \(f^*(\operatorname{R}^1k_*(k^*F))\) is a sheaf equal to \(1\) outside \(X_s\) and constant on \(X_s\)). It then follows from ({*}{*}{*}) that one has \(\operatorname{R}^1j_*(f^*F{|X-\{x\}})=1\), hence \(\operatorname{prof}_x(f^*F)\geqslant 3\).

.8 If one has \(\operatorname{prof}_s(F)\geqslant 1\) and \(\operatorname{prof}_x(f^*F)\geqslant 2\), one considers the sheaf of homogeneous spaces \(G\) defined by the exact sequence

\[ 1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} k_*k*F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 1. \]

Applying to this exact sequence the exact functor \(g^*\) and using (SGA 4 XII 3.1), one obtains the following commutative diagram whose rows are exact:

\[ \xymatrix@R=5mm{{f^*(k_*k^*F)}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{f^*G}\ar[r]\ar[d]^b&1\\ {j_*(g^*(k_*k^*F))}\ar[r]&{j_*(g^*G)}\ar[r]&{\mathrm{R}^1j_*(g^*F)} \ar[r]&{\mathrm{R}^1j_*(g^*(k_*k^*F)).}} \]

Since \(\operatorname{prof}_x(X_s)\geqslant 2\), the morphism \(b\) is bijective hence \(u\) is surjective and one thus has a map with kernel reduced to the neutral element:

\[ 1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{R}^1j_*(g^*F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{R}^1j_*(g^*(k_*k^*F))=R. \]

Since \(g^*(k_*k^*F)\simeq i_*(f^*F{|X-X_s})\) (because \(g\) is locally \(0\)-acyclic), \(R\) is identified with the first term of the exact sequence ({*}{*}{*}); now one saw in the preceding case that \(R=1\) as soon as one has \(\operatorname{prof}_x(X_s)\geqslant 1\), which proves that \(\operatorname{prof}_x(f^*F)\geqslant 3\) and completes the proof of 1.18.

Q.E.D.

The corollaries that follow are generalizations of (SGA 4 XVI 3.2 and 3.3).

Corollary 1.19

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a flat morphism, with separable fibres, of locally noetherian schemes and \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\). Suppose that for every point \(s\in f(Y)\), the fibre \(Y_s\) is nowhere dense in \(X_s\) and that one of the two following conditions is verified:

  1. the closure of \(f(Y)\) is nowhere dense in \(S\).

  2. \(X_s\) is geometrically unibranch at the points of \(Y_s\).

    Then one has

    \[ \operatorname{prof}_Y(X)\geqslant 2. \]

It follows indeed from the hypothesis made on \(f\) that \(f\) is locally \(0\)-acyclic (SGA 4 XV 4.1). One then applies 1.13. The hypothesis \(Y_s\) nowhere dense in \(X_s\) (resp. \(\overline{f(Y)}\) nowhere dense in \(S\)) is equivalent, by 1.6 b), to the relation \(\operatorname{prof}_{Y_s}(X_s)\geqslant 1\) (resp. \(\operatorname{prof}_{\overline{f(Y)}}(S)\geqslant 1\)). The hypothesis \(X_s\) geometrically unibranch at each point of \(Y_s\) is equivalent to saying that the strict localization of \(X_s\) at a geometric point of \(Y_s\) is irreducible; knowing that \(Y_s\) is nowhere dense in \(X_s\), this obviously entails \(\operatorname{prof}_{Y_s}(X_s)\geqslant 2\), thanks to 1.8. In either case 1.13 indeed gives \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(X)\geqslant 2\).

Corollary 1.20

Let \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a regular morphism (EGA IV 6.8.1) of locally noetherian schemes, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\). Suppose that, for every point \(s\in f(Y)\), one of the following conditions is realized:

  1. One has \(\operatorname{codim}(Y_s, X_s)\geqslant 2\).

  2. One has \(\operatorname{codim}(Y_s, X_s)\geqslant 1\) and \(\operatorname{prof}_s(S)\geqslant 1\).

  3. One has \(\operatorname{prof}\operatorname{hop}_s(S)\geqslant 3\).

Then one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}\operatorname{hop}_Y(X)\geqslant 3. \]

This follows indeed from 1.18, given that hypothesis a) implies \(\operatorname{prof}\operatorname{hop}_{Y_s}(X_s)\geqslant 3\) (cf. 1.11), and that the condition \(\operatorname{codim}(Y_s, X_s)\geqslant 1\) obviously implies \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(X)\geqslant 2\).

blocks.html: 1 Cohomological and homotopic depth

Let us fix the following notation. Let \(X\) be a scheme1, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\), \(U\) the complementary open set and \(i:Y=X-U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical immersion. Let \(\underline{\Gamma}_Y\) be the functor which, to an abelian sheaf on \(X\), associates the "sheaf of sections with support in \(Y\)", that is to say \(\underline{\Gamma}_Y=i_*i^!\) (cf. SGA 4 IV 3.8 and VIII 6.6), and \(\Gamma_Y\) the functor \(\Gamma\cdot \underline{\Gamma}_Y\) (where \(\Gamma\) is the "global sections" functor). Consider the derived category \(D^+(X)\) and the derived functor \(\operatorname{R}\underline{\Gamma}_Y\) (resp. \(\operatorname{R}\Gamma_Y\)) of \(\underline{\Gamma}_Y\) (resp. of \(\Gamma_Y\)) (cf. [3]). Given a complex of abelian sheaves \(F\) on \(X\), with degrees bounded below, one can regard it as an element of \(D^+(X)\); we shall then denote by \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F)\) the \(p\)-th cohomology sheaf of \(\operatorname{R}\underline{\Gamma}_Y(F)\) and by \(\operatorname{H}_Y^p(X, F)\) the \(p\)-th cohomology group of \(\operatorname{R}\Gamma_Y(F)\). The results of (SGA 4 V 4.3 and 4.4) extend trivially to \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F)\) and \(\operatorname{H}_Y^p(X, F)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.1

Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\), \(U\) the complementary open set and \(i:U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical immersion. Let \(F\) denote either a sheaf of sets on \(X\), or a sheaf of groups on \(X\), or a complex of abelian sheaves on \(X\), with degrees bounded below. Let us fix the following notation: if \(X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is a morphism, \(U'\) and \(F'\) denote the inverse images of \(U\) and \(F\) on \(X'\); furthermore, if \(\overline{y}\) is a geometric point of \(X\), \(\overline{X}\) denotes the strict localization of \(X\) at \(\overline{y}\) and \(\overline{U}\) and \(\overline{F}\) the inverse images of \(U\) and \(F\) in \(\overline{X}\).

\(1^\circ)\) Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets on \(X\) and \(n\) an integer \(\leqslant 2\); then the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) The canonical morphism

\[ F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*i^*F \]

is injective if \(n\geqslant 1\), bijective if \(n\geqslant 2\).

(ii) For every scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(X', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(U', F') \]

is injective if \(n\geqslant 1\), bijective if \(n\geqslant 2\).

Suppose moreover that the open set \(U\) is retrocompact in \(X\); then the preceding conditions are equivalent to the following:

(iii) For every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(\overline{X}, \overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(\overline{U}, \overline{F}) \]

is injective if \(n\geqslant 1\), and bijective if \(n\geqslant 2\).

\(2^\circ)\) Let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups on \(X\) and \(n\) an integer \(\leqslant 3\); then the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) The canonical morphism

\[ F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*i^*F \]

is injective if \(n\geqslant 1\), bijective if \(n\geqslant 2\), and if \(n\geqslant 3\), in addition to the preceding conditions, the sheaf of pointed sets \(\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F)\) is zero.

(ii) For every scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(X', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(U', F') \]

is injective if \(n\geqslant 1\), bijective if \(n\geqslant 2\), and moreover the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^1(X', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^1(U', F') \]

is injective if \(n\geqslant 2\), bijective if \(n\geqslant 3\).

(ii bis) Identical to (ii), except in the case \(n=2\) where one only assumes \(\operatorname{H}^0(X', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(U', F')\) bijective.

Suppose moreover that \(U\) is retrocompact in \(X\); then the preceding conditions are also equivalent to the following:

(iii) For every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(\overline{X}, \overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(\overline{U}, \overline{F}) \]

is injective if \(n\geqslant 1\), bijective if \(n\geqslant 2\); finally if \(n\geqslant 3\), in addition to the preceding conditions, \(\operatorname{H}^1(\overline{U}, \overline{F})\) is zero.

\(3^\circ)\) Let \(F\) be a complex of abelian sheaves, with degrees bounded below, and \(n\) an integer; then the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) One has \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F)=0\) for \(p<n\) (cf. 1.0).

(ii) For every scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^p(X', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^p(U', F') \]

is bijective for \(p<n-1\), injective for \(p=n-1\).

Suppose \(U\) retrocompact in \(X\); then the preceding conditions are also equivalent to the following:

(iii) For every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^p(\overline{X}, \overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^p(\overline{U}, \overline{F}) \]

is bijective for \(p<n-1\), injective for \(p=n-1\).

In the case where \(F\) is an abelian sheaf and \(n\geqslant 2\), conditions (i) and (ii) are also equivalent to the following:

(ii bis) For every scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^p(X', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^p(U', F') \]

is bijective for \(p<n-1\).

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

\(1^\circ)\) It is clear that (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (ii). Let us show that, if \(U\) is retrocompact in \(X\), (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (iii). Indeed, (i) amounts to saying that, for every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(X\), the morphism \(F_{\overline{y}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (i_*i^*F)_{\overline{y}}\) is injective if \(n\leqslant 1\) and bijective if \(n\leqslant 2\) (SGA 4 VIII 3.6). Since this morphism is in any case bijective when \(\overline{y}\) is a geometric point of \(U\), one may restrict to the geometric points \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y\). Now it follows from the fact that \(i\) is quasi-compact and from (SGA 4 VIII 5.3) that the morphism

\[ F_{\overline{y}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (i_*i^*F)_{\overline{y}} \]

is canonically identified with the morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(\overline{X}, \overline{F})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(\overline{U}, \overline{F}), \]

whence the equivalence of (i) and (iii).

\(2^\circ)\) (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). The assertions on \(\operatorname{H}^0\) follow from \(1^\circ)\). Let then \(i'\) be the canonical immersion of \(U'\) in \(X'\); the assertions on \(\operatorname{H}^1\) follow from the exact sequence (SGA 4 XII 3.2)

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^1(X', i'_*i'^*F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^1(U', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(X', \operatorname{R}^1i'_*(i'^*F')). \]

(ii bis) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i). By \(1^\circ)\), it suffices to show that, for \(n\geqslant 3\), one has \(\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F)=0\). Now \(\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F)\) is the sheaf associated to the presheaf \(X'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \operatorname{H}^1(U', F')\), that is to say, by hypothesis, the sheaf associated to the presheaf \(X'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \operatorname{H}^1(X', F')\), which is zero.

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (iii). Taking \(1^\circ)\) into account, the only thing that remains to be seen is that the relation \(\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F)=0\) is equivalent to the fact that \(\operatorname{H}^1(\overline{U}, \overline{F})=0\) for every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y\). Since \(\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F))\) is zero outside \(Y\), it amounts to the same to say that \(\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F)=0\) or that \((\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F))_{\overline{y}}=0\) for every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y\). It then suffices to note that, \(i\) being quasi-compact, one has \((\operatorname{R}^1i_*(i^*F))_{\overline{y}}=\operatorname{H}^1(\overline{U}, \overline{F})\) (SGA 4 VIII 5.3).

\(3^\circ)\) (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). Let \(X'\) be a scheme étale over \(X\); one has the exact sequence (SGA 4 V 4.5)

\[ \tag{*} {\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^p_{Y'}(X', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^p(X', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^p(U', F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}}; \]

hence (ii) is equivalent to \(H^p_{Y'}(X', F')=0\) for \(p<n\) and for every scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\). Consider then the spectral sequence

\[ \operatorname{E}_2^{pq}=\operatorname{H}^p(X', {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^q(F))\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_{Y'}^*(X', F'); \]

by hypothesis, \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^q(F)=0\) for \(q<n\), whence \(\operatorname{E}_2^{pq}=0\) for \(p+q<n\) and consequently \(\operatorname{H}_{Y'}^p(X', F')=0\) for \(p<n\).

(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i). The sheaf \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F)\) is associated to the presheaf \(X'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \operatorname{H}_{Y'}^p(X', F')\); since we have already remarked that (ii) is equivalent to the relation \(\operatorname{H}_{Y'}^p(X', F')=0\) for \(p<n\) and for every scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\), one indeed has \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F)=0\) for \(p<n\).

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (iii). The sheaves \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F)\) are concentrated on \(Y\); consequently it amounts to the same to say that \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F)=0\) or to say that, for every geometric point \(\overline{y}\) of \(Y\), the stalk \(({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F))_{\overline{y}}\) is zero. Now, \(i\) being quasi-compact, one deduces from (SGA 4 VIII 5.2) that one has \(({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^p(F))_{\overline{y}}={\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_{\overline{Y}}^p(\overline{X}, \overline{F})\). The equivalence of (i) and (iii) follows, taking into account the analogue on \(\overline{X}\) of the exact sequence (*).

(ii bis) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii) in the case where \(F\) is an abelian sheaf. The only thing that remains to be shown is that \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^{n-1}(F)=0\). Now, for \(n>2\), the sheaf \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^{n-1}(F)\) is associated to the presheaf \(X'\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \operatorname{H}^{n-2}(U', F')=\operatorname{H}^{n-2}(X', F')\) hence is indeed zero. The case \(n=2\) follows from the fact that \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^1(F)\) is the cokernel of the morphism \(F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_*i^*F\).

Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.2

The notation is that of 1.1. One says that \(F\) is of étale \(Y\)-depth \(\geqslant n\) and one writes

\[ \operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant n \]

if \(F\) satisfies the equivalent conditions (i) and (ii) of 1.1. If \(F\) is a complex of abelian sheaves, one calls the étale \(Y\)-depth of \(F\) the supremum of the \(n\) for which \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant n\); one will use the same notation if \(F\) is a sheaf of sets resp. of not necessarily commutative groups (so that one then has \(0\leqslant \operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\leqslant 2\) resp. \(0\leqslant \operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\leqslant 3\), when the context does not allow confusion as to which of the three variants considered here is being used).

If \(\mathbf{L}\) is a set of prime numbers, one says that \(X\) is of étale \(Y\)-depth for \(\mathbf{L} \geqslant n\) and one writes

\[ \operatorname{prof}_Y^{\mathbf{L}}(X)\geqslant n \]

if, for every constant sheaf of the form \(\mathbf{Z}/\ell\mathbf{Z}\) with \(\ell\in \mathbf{L}\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(\mathbf{Z}/\ell\mathbf{Z})\geqslant n\). One defines in the obvious way the étale \(Y\)-depth for \(\mathbf{L}\) of \(X\). If \(\mathbf{L}=\mathbf{P}\), the set of all prime numbers, and if there is no risk of confusion with the notation of (EGA IV 5.7.1) (relative to the case where \(F=\mathscr{O}_X\)), one omits \(\mathbf{L}\) in the notation; otherwise one writes \(\operatorname{prof} {\textup{ét}}(X)\).

Finally one says that \(X\) is of homotopic \(Y\)-depth for \(\mathbf{L} \geqslant 3\) and one writes

\[ \operatorname{prof} \operatorname{hop}_Y^{\mathbf{L}}(X)\geqslant 3 \]

if, for every finite constant sheaf of \(\mathbf{L}\)-groups \(F\) on \(X\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant 3\). If \(\mathbf{L}=\mathbf{P}\), one omits \(\mathbf{L}\) in the notation.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.3

Under the conditions of 1.1, if \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant n\), then, for every closed set \(Z\) of \(Y\), one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_Z(F)\geqslant n. \]

Let us carry out the argument, for example, in the case where \(F\) is a complex of abelian sheaves with degrees bounded below. One uses 1.1 \(3^\circ)\) (ii). Let \(V=X-Z\) and consider, for every integer \(p\), the sequence of morphisms
\[ \operatorname{H}^p(X, F)\xrightarrow{f} \operatorname{H}^p(V, F)\xrightarrow{g} \operatorname{H}^p(U, F). \]

By hypothesis \(g\) and \(f\circ g\) are bijective for \(p<n-1\) and injective for \(p=n-1\); the same is therefore true of \(f\). Since the argument is valid when one replaces \(X\) by a scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\), this proves 1.3.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.4

The notation is that of 1.1 \(2^\circ)\). If \(X'\) is a scheme over \(X\), let us denote by \(\Phi'\) the functor which associates to an étale covering of \(X'\) its restriction to \(U'\), and by \(\Phi'_{F'}\) the functor which associates to a torsor2 under \(F'\) its restriction to \(U'\). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) One has \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant 1\) (resp. \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant 2\), resp. \(\operatorname{prof}_Y(F)\geqslant 3\)).

(ii) For every scheme \(X'\) étale over \(X\), the functor \(\Phi'_{F'}\) is faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. an equivalence of categories).

In particular, in order that one have \(\operatorname{prof}_Y({X})\geqslant 1\) (resp. \(\operatorname{prof}_Y({X})\geqslant 2\), resp. \(\operatorname{prof} \operatorname{hop}_Y(X)\geqslant 3\)), it is necessary and sufficient that the functor \(\Phi'\) be faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. an equivalence of categories).
