    \[ H^p((T_W)', G_q)=0 \text{ for } p>\dim g^{-1}(\overline{\{s\}})=d, \]

    whence condition a).

  2. \(2^\circ)\)

    Let us show that one can choose \(V\) in such a way that one has \((G_r)_{\overline{s}'} \neq 0\). By (i), there exists a constructible sheaf \(I\), defined over a finite separable extension \(K\) of \(k(x)\), whose inverse image on \((X')_{(\operatorname{Spec} K)}\) is isomorphic to \(F{|(X')_{(\operatorname{Spec} K)}}\). By 3.3, one finds a finite separable extension \(L\) of \(K\) such that, if \(v:\operatorname{Spec} L \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec} k(s)\) denotes the canonical morphism, one has \(R^r v_*(I) \neq 0\). As the morphism \(S'_s \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec} k(s)\) is regular, one has by (SGA 4 XIX 4.2):

    \[ R^rv_*'(F{|(\operatorname{Spec} L)'}) \simeq (R^rv_*(I))' \neq 0. \]

    By lemma 3.4, one can find an affine scheme \(X_2\) étale over \(X\), and a point \(x_2\) of \(X_2\) over \(x\), such that \(L\) is \(k(x)\)-isomorphic to \(k(x_2)\) and one may suppose \(X_2\) over \(U\). As \(x\) is a maximal point of \(Z\), one has

    \[ \operatorname{Spec} L \simeq \varprojlim_V Z_V, \]

    where \(V\) runs through the affine open neighbourhoods of \(x_2\). One deduces from this by passage to the limit (SGA 4 VII 5.8), after restriction to the geometric fibre at \(\overline{s}'\):

    \[ (R^rv_*'(F{|\operatorname{Spec} I})')_{\overline{s}'} = \varinjlim_V (R^ru_*'(F)_{\overline{s}'}, \]

    which indeed shows that one can find \(V\) such that one has \((G_r)_{\overline{s}'} \neq 0\).

  3. \(3^\circ)\)

    The scheme \(V\) having been chosen in \(2^\circ)\), let us show that one can choose the scheme \(W\) in such a way that one has

    \[ E^{dr}_2 = H^d((T_W)', G_r) \neq 0. \]

    By (ii), there exists a constructible sheaf \(J\), defined over a finite separable extension \(K\) of \(k(s)\), whose inverse image on \((S')_{(\operatorname{Spec} K)}\) is isomorphic to \({G_r}{|(S')_{(\operatorname{Spec} K)}}\). By lemma 3.2, one can find a finite separable extension \(L\) of \(K\), such that one has \(H^d(\operatorname{Spec} L, J) \neq 0\). As the morphism \((S')_{(\operatorname{Spec} L)} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec} L\) is acyclic (SGA 4 XIX 4.1 and XV 1.10 and 1.16), one has

    \[ H^d((\operatorname{Spec} L)', {G_r}{|(S')_{(\operatorname{Spec} L)'}}) = H^d(\operatorname{Spec} L, J) \neq 0. \]

    By 3.4, one can find an affine scheme \(U_1\) étale over \(U\), and a point \(s_1\) over \(s\), such that \(k(s_1)\) is \(k(s)\)-isomorphic to \(L\). Now, \(s\) being a maximal point of \(T_U\), one has

    \[ \operatorname{Spec} L \simeq \varprojlim_W T_W, \]

    where \(W\) runs through the affine open neighbourhoods of \(s_1\). One deduces from this that \((\operatorname{Spec} L)' \simeq \varprojlim_W(T_W)'\), and by passage to the limit (SGA 4 VII 5.8):

    \[ H^d((\operatorname{Spec} L)', {G_r}{|(\operatorname{Spec} L)'}) \simeq \varinjlim_W H^d((T_W)', {G_r}{|(T_W)'}); \]

    consequently one can find \(W\) such that one has

    \[ H^d((T_W)', {G_r}{|(T_W)'})\neq 0, \]

    which completes the proof of the theorem.

Corollary 3.6

The hypotheses concerning \(S, S', f, f', m\) are those of 3.5. Let us now denote by \(F\) a complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules on \(X'\), bounded below in degree and with constructible cohomology, and whose cohomology sheaves satisfy conditions (i) and (ii) of 3.5. Let \(n\) be an integer, and suppose that, for every scheme \(X_1\) étale over \(X\), affine, one has

\[ H^i(X'_1, F)=0\text{ for } i>n. \]

Then, if \(\overline{x}'\) is a geometric point over a point \(x'\) of \(X'\), such that one has, for an integer \(j\), \((\underline{H}^j(F))_{\overline{x}'} \neq 0\), one has

\[ \delta(x')\leqslant n-j. \]

Let \(T'\) be the set of points of \(X'\) where the conclusion of 3.7 fails and suppose \(T' \neq \emptyset\); let \(T=f(T')\), \(x\) a maximal point of \(T\) and \(x'\) a point of \(X'\) over \(x\). Let \(j\) be the largest integer such that one has \((\underline{H}^j(F))_{\overline{x}'} \neq 0\); one therefore has \(r=\delta(x)>n-j\). Let \(Z'_q\) be the set of points where the geometric fibre of \(\underline{H}^q(F)\) is \(=0\) and \(Z_q =h(Z'_q)\); one sees as in the proof of 3.5 that \(Z_q\) is constructible. One obviously has \(Z'_q=\emptyset\) for \(q>n\) and for \(q\) sufficiently small. The other values of \(q\) are distributed into three subsets. Let

\[ Q_1=\{q\mid x\in Z_q\text{ and a generization of }x, \text{ distinct from }x, \not\in Z_q\}. \]

One has \(j \in Q_1\) and one can find an affine open neighbourhood \(U_1\) of \(x\), such that, for every \(q \in Q_1, U_1 \cap Z_q\) is an irreducible closed set with generic point \(x\). If \(q \in Q_1\), one has

\[ \tag{*} \delta(\underline{H}^q(F){|U_1'})=\delta(x)\quad\text{(for the definition of $\delta(\underline{H}^q(F))$ cf. 3.1)}. \]

Let

\[ Q_2=\{q\mid\text{no generization of $x$ belongs to }Z_q\}. \]

Then, if \(j<q\leqslant n\), one has \(q\in Q_2\), and one can find an affine open neighbourhood \(U_2\) of \(x\), such that, for every \(q \in Q_2\), one has \(Z_q \cap U_2 = \emptyset\); one thus has

\[ \tag{**} \underline{H}^q(F){|U'_2}=0\text{ for }q\in Q_2. \]

Let finally

\[ Q_3= \{q\mid Z_q \text{ contains strict generizations of }x\}. \]

Then one can find an affine open neighbourhood of \(x\), \(U_3\), such that, for every \(q \in Q_3\), all the maximal points of \(Z_q \cap U_3\) are generizations of \(x\). If \(q \in Q_3\), one has

\[ \tag{{*}{*}{*}} \delta(\underline{H}^q(F){|U'_3})\leqslant n-q. \]

For every scheme \(X_1\) étale over \(U_1 \cap U_2 \cap {}_3\), affine, let us consider the hypercohomology spectral sequence

\[ E^{pq}_2= H^p(X_1', \underline{H}^q(F)) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} H^*(X'_1, F). \]

One has \(E^{pq}_2=0\) for \(q>\) by (**). One has \(E^{pq}_2=0\) for \(p+q \geqslant r+j\) except perhaps for \(p=r, q=j\). Indeed it is clear if \(q\in Q_2\); if \(q\in Q_1\), one then has \(p>r\) except if \(p=r, q=j\) and this follows from 3.1 taking into account (*); finally if \(q\in Q_3\), as \(r>n-j\), one has \(p>n-q\) and the assertion follows from 3.1 taking into account ({*}{*}{*}). Since \(H^{r+j}(X_1', F)=0\), it follows from the spectral sequence that one has

\[ H^r(X_1', \underline{H}^j(F))=0; \]

now this entails, by 3.5, \(\delta(x)<r\), which is absurd.

Corollary 3.7

Let \(S\) be an excellent strictly local scheme of characteristic zero, \(f:X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism locally of finite type, \(m\) a power of a prime number, \(F\) a complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules on \(X\), bounded below, with constructible cohomology, and \(n\) an integer. Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. For every scheme \(X_1\) étale over \(X\), affine, one has

    \[ H^i(X_1, F)=0\text{ for }i>n. \]

  2. For every geometric point \(\overline{x}\) over the point \(x\) of \(X\), and for every integer \(j\) such that one has \((\underline{H}^j(F))_{\overline{x}} \neq 0\), one has

    \[ \delta(x) \leqslant n-j. \]

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii) is the particular case of 3.6 obtained by taking \(S=S'\).

(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i) follows immediately from 3.1, using the hypercohomology spectral sequence

\[ H^p(X_1, \underline{H}^q(F)) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} H^*(X_1, F). \]

blocks.html: 3 Converse of the affine Lefschetz theorem

The present subsection will be used in n° 4 to prove a converse to the "Lefschetz theorem"; a reader who is interested only in the direct part of the said theorem may therefore omit reading the present subsection.
Let us recall the statement of the affine Lefschetz theorem (SGA 4 XIX 6.1 bis):

Let \(S\) be an excellent strictly local scheme of characteristic zero, \(f:X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) an affine morphism of finite type and \(F\) a torsion sheaf on \(X\). Then, if one sets
\[ \delta(F)=\sup\{\delta(x)|x\in X \text{ and } F_{\overline{x}} \neq 0\}, \]
one has
\[ H^q(X, F)=0 \text{ for } q>\delta(F). \]
Before stating the converse, let us prove a few lemmas.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.2

Let \(K\) be a field, \(\ell\) a prime number distinct from the characteristic of \(K\) and \(F\) a constructible, nonzero sheaf of \(\ell\)-torsion on \(K\). Suppose that the \(\ell\)-cohomological dimension of \(K\) (SGA 4 X 1) is equal to \(n\) (this is realized for example if \(K\) is the field of fractions of an excellent integral strictly local ring of characteristic zero and dimension \(n\) (SGA 4 XIX 6.3), or if \(K\) is a finitely generated extension of transcendence degree \(n\) of a separably closed field (SGA 4 X 2.1)). Then one can find a finite separable extension \(L\) of \(K\), such that one has:

\[ H^n(L, F{|L}) \neq 0. \]

One can find a finite extension \(K'\) of \(K\), such that the restrictions of \(F\) and of \(\mu_\ell\) to \(\operatorname{Spec} K'\) are constant sheaves. One then has \(\operatorname{cd}_{\ell}(K') = \operatorname{cd}_{\ell}(K) = n\) (SGA 4 X 2.1), and it follows from ([2] II §3 Prop. 4 (iii)) that one can find a finite extension \(L\) of \(K'\) such that one has
\[ H^n(L, \mu_{\ell})\neq 0, \quad\text {i.e. } H^n(L, \mathbf{Z}/\ell\mathbf{Z}) \neq 0. \]

Now the functor \(H^n(L,\cdot)\) is right exact on the category of sheaves of \(\ell\)-torsion, since \(\operatorname{cd}_{\ell}(L)=n\); as \(F\) admits a quotient isomorphic to \(\mathbf{Z}/\ell\mathbf{Z}\), one also has \(H^n(L, F{|L}) \neq 0\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.3

Let \(k\) be a field, \(K\) a finitely generated extension of transcendence degree \(n\) of \(k\), \(F\) a constructible, nonzero sheaf of \(\ell\)-torsion on \(K\), with \(\ell\) prime to the characteristic of \(k\). Then one can find a finite separable extension \(L\) of \(K\) such that, if \(u: \operatorname{Spec} L \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec} k\) denotes the canonical morphism, one has

\[ R^n u_*(F{|\operatorname{Spec} L}) \neq 0. \]

When the field \(k\) is separably closed, the corollary is a particular case of 3.2. In the general case, one can find a finite separable extension \(k_1\) of \(k'\) such that the irreducible components of \(K \otimes_k k_1\) are geometrically irreducible (EGA IV 4.5.11); let \(K_1\) be one of them. If \(k'\) is a separable closure of \(k_1\), then \(K' = K_1 \otimes_{k_1} k'\) is a field, and one has by (EGA IV 4.2)
\[ \deg \operatorname{tr} K'/k' = \deg \operatorname{tr} K/k = n. \]

It then follows from 3.2 that one can find a finite separable extension \(L'\) of \(K'\) such that one has \(H^n(L', F{|L'}) \neq 0\). But one has \(k' = \varinjlim_i k_i\), where \(k_i\) runs through the finite extensions of \(k_1\) contained in \(k'\), and consequently \(K' = \varinjlim_i (k_i \otimes_{k_1} K_1)\). It follows that one can find an index \(i\) and a finite separable extension \(L\) of \(k_i \otimes_{k_1} K_1 =K_i\), such that one has \(L' \simeq L \otimes_{K_i} K'\). The extension \(L\) of \(K\) answers the question; indeed it follows from the commutative diagram
\[ \xymatrix{ &{\mathrm{Spec}\,L}\ar[dl]_v\ar[dr]^u&\\ {\mathrm{Spec}\,k_i} \ar[rr]^w &{}&{\mathrm{Spec}\,k\,,} } \]
with \(w\) finite hence \(R^q w_* = 0\) if \(q>0\), that one has
\[ R^n u_*(F{|\operatorname{Spec} L}) \simeq w_*(R^n v_*(F{|\operatorname{Spec} L})). \]
Now \(R^nv_*(F{|\operatorname{Spec} L}) eq 0\), since \(H^n(L', F{|L'}) eq 0\); one therefore also has \(R^nu_*(F{|\operatorname{Spec} L}) eq 0\).
Let us recall the following well-known lemma (cf. \(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{III}}\) 10.3.1.2 and EGA IV 18.2.3):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.4

Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(x\) a point of \(X\), \(K\) a finite separable extension of \(k(x)\). Then there exists an affine scheme \(X_1\) étale over \(X\), and a point \(x_1 \in X_1\) over \(x\), such that \(k(x_1)\) is \(k(x)\)-isomorphic to \(K\).
We will use in n° 4 the following technical form of the converse of 3.1.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.5

Let there be given a cartesian square

\[ \xymatrix{ X^{\prime}\ar[r]^h\ar[d]_{f^{\prime}} &X\ar[d]^f\\ S^{\prime}\ar[r]^g &S\,, } \]

where the schemes \(S\) and \(S'\) are excellent strictly local of characteristic zero, the morphism \(f\) locally of finite type, \(g\) regular (EGA IV 6.8.1) surjective, the closed fibre of \(g\) reduced to the closed point of \(S'\). Given an \(S\)-scheme \(X_1\) (resp. an \(S\)-morphism \(f_1\), etc.), we will denote by \(X_1'\) (resp. \(f_1'\), etc.) the scheme \(X_1 \times_S S'\) (resp. the morphism \((f_1)_{(S')}\), etc.). Let \(F\) be a constructible sheaf of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules on \(X'\) (\(m\) a power of a prime number \(\ell\)), satisfying the following conditions:

  1. For every point \(x \in X\), one can find a finite separable extension \(K\) of \(k(x)\) such that the restriction of \(F\) to the fibre \((X')_{(\operatorname{Spec} K)}\) arises by inverse image from a constructible sheaf on \(\operatorname{Spec} K\).

  2. For every morphism \(f_1: X_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), with \(X_1\) étale over \(X\), affine, for every point \(s \in S\) and for every integer \(q>0\), one can find a finite separable extension \(K\) of \(k(s)\) such that the restriction of \(R^q f'_{1*}(F|X'_1)\) to the fibre \(S'_{(\operatorname{Spec} K)}\) arises by inverse image from a constructible sheaf on \(\operatorname{Spec} K\).

Let \(n\) be an integer, and suppose that for every scheme \(X_1\) étale over \(X\), affine, one has

\[ H^i(X_1', F)=0 \text{ for } i>n. \]

Then, if \(\overline{x}'\) is a geometric point over the point \(x' \in X'\), such that \(F_{\overline{x}'} \neq 0\), one has

\[ \delta(x') \leqslant n. \]

Let \(Z'\) be the set of points \(x'\) of \(X'\) such that one has \(F_{\overline{x}'} = 0\). Then, if \(Z=h(Z')\), one has by (i) \(Z' = h^{-1}(Z)\); let \(x' \in X'\), \(x=h(x')\), \(s'=f'(x')\), \(s=f(x)\). It follows from 2.1.1 and from the fact that the function \(\delta\) decreases by specialization, that it suffices to prove the inequality \(\delta(x') \leqslant n\) when \(x\) is a maximal point of \(Z\) and \(x'\) such that one has
\[ r=\deg \operatorname{tr} k(x)/k(s) = \deg \operatorname{tr} k(x')/k(s') \text{ and }d=\dim \overline{\{s\}} = \dim\overline{\{s'\}} \]
Let \(x'\) be such a point; it suffices to show that one can find an affine scheme \(X_1\) étale over \(X\), such that one has
\[ H^{d+r}(X_1', F) \neq 0. \]

The set \(Z'\) is constructible (SGA 4 IX 2.4), hence so is \(Z\) (EGA IV 1.9.12); one may then suppose, after possibly restricting \(X\) to a neighbourhood of \(x\), that \(Z\) is an irreducible closed set with generic point \(x\). Let \(T=f(Z)\); \(T\) is a constructible set contained in \(\overline{\{s\}}\); one can therefore find an affine open set \(U\) of \(S\), such that \(s \in U\) and that \(T \cap U = T_U\) is an irreducible closed set of \(U\) with generic point \(s\).

Let then \(V\) be an affine scheme étale over \(X\), whose image in \(X\) contains \(x\) and whose image in \(S\) is contained in \(U\); let \(Z_V\) be the inverse image of \(Z\) in \(V\) and \(u: Z_V \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} T_U\) the canonical morphism. Let \(W\) be an affine scheme étale over \(U\), we then denote by \(T_W\) the inverse image of \(T_U\) in \(W\) and let \(X_1 = W\times_U V\). As \(F\) is zero outside \(Z'\), one has the spectral sequence
\[ E^{pq}_2 = H^p((T_W)', R^qu_*'(F)) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} H^*(X'_1, F). \]
We are going to show that one can choose \(V\) and \(W\) in such a way that one has

1. \(E^{pq}_2=0\) for \(p>d\) and for \(q>r\).

  2. \(E^{dr}_2 \neq 0\).
It will then follow from the spectral sequence the relation \(H^{d+r}(X_1', F) \neq 0\).

1. \(1^\circ)\)

    Set \(G_q = R^q u'_*(F)\); then one has:

    \[ (G_q)_{\overline{s}'}=H^q((Z_V)'_{\overline{s}'}, F_{\overline{s}'}), \]

    for \(s'\) is a maximal point of \((T_U)'\). As the fibre \((Z_V)'_{\overline{s}'}\) is an affine scheme of finite type of dimension \(r\) over a separably closed field, it follows from 3.1 that one has

    \[ (G_q)_{\overline{s}'}=0 \text{ for } q>r. \]

    For \(q>r\), let \(Y'_q\) be the set of points of \((T_U)'\) where the geometric fibre of \(G_q\) is \(\neq 0\) and \(Y_q=g(Y'_q)\); then one has \(Y'_q = g^{-1}(Y_q)\) by (ii), hence \(Y_q\) is a constructible subset of \(T_U\) (SGA 4 XIX 5.1 and EGA 1.9.12) which does not contain \(s\); after possibly restricting \(U\) to an open neighbourhood of \(s\), one may suppose that one has \(G_q=0\) for \(q>r\), hence \(E_2^{pq}=0\) for \(q>r\).

    Moreover, as \((T_W)'\) is an affine scheme of finite type over \(g^{-1}(\overline{\{s\}})\), one has whatever \(q\) (cf. 3.1):

    \[ H^p((T_W)', G_q)=0 \text{ for } p>\dim g^{-1}(\overline{\{s\}})=d, \]

    whence condition a).

  2. \(2^\circ)\)

    Let us show that one can choose \(V\) in such a way that one has \((G_r)_{\overline{s}'} \neq 0\). By (i), there exists a constructible sheaf \(I\), defined over a finite separable extension \(K\) of \(k(x)\), whose inverse image on \((X')_{(\operatorname{Spec} K)}\) is isomorphic to \(F{|(X')_{(\operatorname{Spec} K)}}\). By 3.3, one finds a finite separable extension \(L\) of \(K\) such that, if \(v:\operatorname{Spec} L \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec} k(s)\) denotes the canonical morphism, one has \(R^r v_*(I) \neq 0\). As the morphism \(S'_s \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec} k(s)\) is regular, one has by (SGA 4 XIX 4.2):

    \[ R^rv_*'(F{|(\operatorname{Spec} L)'}) \simeq (R^rv_*(I))' \neq 0. \]

    By lemma 3.4, one can find an affine scheme \(X_2\) étale over \(X\), and a point \(x_2\) of \(X_2\) over \(x\), such that \(L\) is \(k(x)\)-isomorphic to \(k(x_2)\) and one may suppose \(X_2\) over \(U\). As \(x\) is a maximal point of \(Z\), one has

    \[ \operatorname{Spec} L \simeq \varprojlim_V Z_V, \]

    where \(V\) runs through the affine open neighbourhoods of \(x_2\). One deduces from this by passage to the limit (SGA 4 VII 5.8), after restriction to the geometric fibre at \(\overline{s}'\):

    \[ (R^rv_*'(F{|\operatorname{Spec} I})')_{\overline{s}'} = \varinjlim_V (R^ru_*'(F)_{\overline{s}'}, \]

    which indeed shows that one can find \(V\) such that one has \((G_r)_{\overline{s}'} \neq 0\).

  3. \(3^\circ)\)

    The scheme \(V\) having been chosen in \(2^\circ)\), let us show that one can choose the scheme \(W\) in such a way that one has

    \[ E^{dr}_2 = H^d((T_W)', G_r) \neq 0. \]

    By (ii), there exists a constructible sheaf \(J\), defined over a finite separable extension \(K\) of \(k(s)\), whose inverse image on \((S')_{(\operatorname{Spec} K)}\) is isomorphic to \({G_r}{|(S')_{(\operatorname{Spec} K)}}\). By lemma 3.2, one can find a finite separable extension \(L\) of \(K\), such that one has \(H^d(\operatorname{Spec} L, J) \neq 0\). As the morphism \((S')_{(\operatorname{Spec} L)} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Spec} L\) is acyclic (SGA 4 XIX 4.1 and XV 1.10 and 1.16), one has

    \[ H^d((\operatorname{Spec} L)', {G_r}{|(S')_{(\operatorname{Spec} L)'}}) = H^d(\operatorname{Spec} L, J) \neq 0. \]

    By 3.4, one can find an affine scheme \(U_1\) étale over \(U\), and a point \(s_1\) over \(s\), such that \(k(s_1)\) is \(k(s)\)-isomorphic to \(L\). Now, \(s\) being a maximal point of \(T_U\), one has

    \[ \operatorname{Spec} L \simeq \varprojlim_W T_W, \]

    where \(W\) runs through the affine open neighbourhoods of \(s_1\). One deduces from this that \((\operatorname{Spec} L)' \simeq \varprojlim_W(T_W)'\), and by passage to the limit (SGA 4 VII 5.8):

    \[ H^d((\operatorname{Spec} L)', {G_r}{|(\operatorname{Spec} L)'}) \simeq \varinjlim_W H^d((T_W)', {G_r}{|(T_W)'}); \]

    consequently one can find \(W\) such that one has

    \[ H^d((T_W)', {G_r}{|(T_W)'})\neq 0, \]

    which completes the proof of the theorem.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.6

The hypotheses concerning \(S, S', f, f', m\) are those of 3.5. Let us now denote by \(F\) a complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules on \(X'\), bounded below in degree and with constructible cohomology, and whose cohomology sheaves satisfy conditions (i) and (ii) of 3.5. Let \(n\) be an integer, and suppose that, for every scheme \(X_1\) étale over \(X\), affine, one has

\[ H^i(X'_1, F)=0\text{ for } i>n. \]

Then, if \(\overline{x}'\) is a geometric point over a point \(x'\) of \(X'\), such that one has, for an integer \(j\), \((\underline{H}^j(F))_{\overline{x}'} \neq 0\), one has

\[ \delta(x')\leqslant n-j. \]

Let \(T'\) be the set of points of \(X'\) where the conclusion of 3.7 fails and suppose \(T' \neq \emptyset\); let \(T=f(T')\), \(x\) a maximal point of \(T\) and \(x'\) a point of \(X'\) over \(x\). Let \(j\) be the largest integer such that one has \((\underline{H}^j(F))_{\overline{x}'} \neq 0\); one therefore has \(r=\delta(x)>n-j\). Let \(Z'_q\) be the set of points where the geometric fibre of \(\underline{H}^q(F)\) is \(=0\) and \(Z_q =h(Z'_q)\); one sees as in the proof of 3.5 that \(Z_q\) is constructible. One obviously has \(Z'_q=\emptyset\) for \(q>n\) and for \(q\) sufficiently small. The other values of \(q\) are distributed into three subsets. Let
\[ Q_1=\{q\mid x\in Z_q\text{ and a generization of }x, \text{ distinct from }x, \not\in Z_q\}. \]

One has \(j \in Q_1\) and one can find an affine open neighbourhood \(U_1\) of \(x\), such that, for every \(q \in Q_1, U_1 \cap Z_q\) is an irreducible closed set with generic point \(x\). If \(q \in Q_1\), one has
\[ \tag{*} \delta(\underline{H}^q(F){|U_1'})=\delta(x)\quad\text{(for the definition of $\delta(\underline{H}^q(F))$ cf. 3.1)}. \]
Let
\[ Q_2=\{q\mid\text{no generization of $x$ belongs to }Z_q\}. \]

Then, if \(j<q\leqslant n\), one has \(q\in Q_2\), and one can find an affine open neighbourhood \(U_2\) of \(x\), such that, for every \(q \in Q_2\), one has \(Z_q \cap U_2 = \emptyset\); one thus has
\[ \tag{**} \underline{H}^q(F){|U'_2}=0\text{ for }q\in Q_2. \]
Let finally
\[ Q_3= \{q\mid Z_q \text{ contains strict generizations of }x\}. \]
Then one can find an affine open neighbourhood of \(x\), \(U_3\), such that, for every \(q \in Q_3\), all the maximal points of \(Z_q \cap U_3\) are generizations of \(x\). If \(q \in Q_3\), one has
\[ \tag{{*}{*}{*}} \delta(\underline{H}^q(F){|U'_3})\leqslant n-q. \]
For every scheme \(X_1\) étale over \(U_1 \cap U_2 \cap {}_3\), affine, let us consider the hypercohomology spectral sequence
\[ E^{pq}_2= H^p(X_1', \underline{H}^q(F)) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} H^*(X'_1, F). \]

One has \(E^{pq}_2=0\) for \(q>\) by (**). One has \(E^{pq}_2=0\) for \(p+q \geqslant r+j\) except perhaps for \(p=r, q=j\). Indeed it is clear if \(q\in Q_2\); if \(q\in Q_1\), one then has \(p>r\) except if \(p=r, q=j\) and this follows from 3.1 taking into account (*); finally if \(q\in Q_3\), as \(r>n-j\), one has \(p>n-q\) and the assertion follows from 3.1 taking into account ({*}{*}{*}). Since \(H^{r+j}(X_1', F)=0\), it follows from the spectral sequence that one has
\[ H^r(X_1', \underline{H}^j(F))=0; \]
now this entails, by 3.5, \(\delta(x)<r\), which is absurd.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.7

Let \(S\) be an excellent strictly local scheme of characteristic zero, \(f:X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism locally of finite type, \(m\) a power of a prime number, \(F\) a complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules on \(X\), bounded below, with constructible cohomology, and \(n\) an integer. Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. For every scheme \(X_1\) étale over \(X\), affine, one has

    \[ H^i(X_1, F)=0\text{ for }i>n. \]

  2. For every geometric point \(\overline{x}\) over the point \(x\) of \(X\), and for every integer \(j\) such that one has \((\underline{H}^j(F))_{\overline{x}} \neq 0\), one has

    \[ \delta(x) \leqslant n-j. \]
(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii) is the particular case of 3.6 obtained by taking \(S=S'\).
(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i) follows immediately from 3.1, using the hypercohomology spectral sequence
\[ H^p(X_1, \underline{H}^q(F)) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} H^*(X_1, F). \]

pages.title: Main theorem and variants
pages.html:
4 Main theorem and variants

4.0

Let \(g\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a separated morphism of finite type, \(T\) a closed subset of \(S\), \(Z=g^{-1}(T)\) and \(F\) a complex of abelian sheaves on \(X\), bounded below in degree. We call \(i\)-th cohomology group of \(F\), with proper support, with support in \(Z\) the group

\[ \operatorname{H}_{Z!}^{i}(X/S, F)=\operatorname{H}_{T}^{i}(S, \operatorname{R}_{!}g(F)), \]

where \(\operatorname{R}_{!}g\) denotes "the direct image with proper support" (SGA 4 XVII). In the particular case where \(g\) is proper, one simply has

\[ \operatorname{H}_{Z!}^{i}(X/S, F)=\operatorname{H}_{Z}^{i}(X, F). \]
Proposition 4.1

Let \(f\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of finite type, \(F\) a complex of abelian sheaves on \(U\), bounded below in degree. Suppose that we have a factorization of \(f\):

\[ \xymatrix@C=15pt{ U\ar[rr]^-{i}\ar[rd]_-{f} & & X\ar[ld]^-{g}\\ & S & } \]

where \(i\) is an open immersion and \(g\) a separated morphism of finite type, and let \(G\) denote a complex of abelian sheaves on \(X\), bounded below in degree, which extends \(F\). Let \(Y\) be a closed subscheme of \(X\) with underlying space \(X-U\), so that we have a commutative diagram:

\[ \xymatrix@C=15pt{ Y\ar[rr]^-{j}\ar[rd]_-{h} & & X\ar[ld]^-{g}\\ & S & } \]

Finally, let \(n\) be an integer and \(T\) a closed subset of \(S\). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. One has \(\operatorname{prof}_{T}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(F))\geqslant n\).

  2. The canonical morphism

    \[ \underline{H}_{T}^{i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}g(G))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\underline{H}_{T}^{i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}h(j^{\ast}G)) \]

    is bijective for \(i<n-1\), injective for \(i=n-1\).

  3. For every scheme \(S^{\prime}\) étale over \(S\), if one denotes by \(X^{\prime}\) (resp. \(f^{\prime}\), resp. etc.) the scheme \(X\times_{S}S^{\prime}\) (resp. the morphism \(f_{(S^{\prime})}\), resp. etc.), the canonical morphism

    \[ \operatorname{H}_{g^{\prime -1}(T^{\prime})!}^{i}(X^{\prime}/S^{\prime}, G^{\prime})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{h^{\prime -1}(T^{\prime})!}^{i}(Y^{\prime}/S^{\prime}, j^{\prime\ast}G^{\prime}) \]

    is bijective for \(i<n-1\), injective for \(i=n-1\).

Consider in the derived category \(\operatorname{D}^{+}(X)\) (cf. [3]) the distinguished triangle

\[ \xymatrix@C=15pt{ & j_{\ast}j^{\ast}G\ar[ld] & \\ i_{!}F\ar[rr] & & G.\ar[lu]} \]

Applying the functor \(\operatorname{R}_{!}g\) to this triangle, one obtains the triangle

\[ \tag{*} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix@C=0pt{ & \mathrm{R}_{!}h(j^{\ast}G)\ar[ld] & \\ \mathrm{R}_{!}f(F)\ar[rr] & & \mathrm{R}_{!}g(G).\ar[lu] } \end{array} \]

Let us show that (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (ii). Indeed, according to definition 1.2, (i) is equivalent to the relation

\[ \underline{H}_{T}^{i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(F))=0\quad\textrm{ for }i<n\textrm{;} \]

now from (*) one deduces the exact sequence of sheaves

\[ \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\underline{H}_{T}^{i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(F))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\underline{H}_{T}^{i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}g(G))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\underline{H}_{T}^{i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}h(j^{\ast}G))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}, \]

whence the equivalence of (i) and (ii).

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (iii). Indeed (i) is equivalent to saying that, for every scheme \(S^{\prime}\) étale over \(S\), one has the relation

\[ \tag{**} {\operatorname{H}_{T^{\prime}}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R}_{!}f^{\prime}(F^{\prime}))=0\quad\textrm{ for }i<n.} \]

Now from (*) one deduces the exact sequence of abelian groups

\[ \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{T^{\prime}}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R}_{!}f^{\prime}(F^{\prime}))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{T^{\prime}}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R}_{!}g^{\prime}(G^{\prime}))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_{T^{\prime}}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R}_{!}h^{\prime}(j^{\prime\ast}G^{\prime}))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\;; \]

taking into account 4.0, this exact sequence is written in the form

\[ \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{T^{\prime}}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R}_{!}f^{\prime}(F^{\prime}))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{g^{\prime -1}(T^{\prime})!}^{i}(X^{\prime}/S^{\prime}, G^{\prime})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{h^{\prime -1}(T^{\prime})!}^{i}(Y^{\prime}/S^{\prime}, j^{\prime\ast}G^{\prime}) \]

The equivalence of (i) and (iii) follows from this, taking into account the form (**) of (i).

4.2.0

When \(f\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is affine, we are going to give local conditions on \(F\) for the conditions (i) to (iii) of 4.1 to be satisfied. In what follows, the schemes considered are excellent schemes of characteristic zero, the sheaves are sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules, where \(m\) is a power of a prime number. If one had resolution of singularities in the sense of (SGA 4 XIX), the results stated, as well as their proofs, would still be valid for excellent schemes of equal characteristic, with \(m\) prime to the characteristic.

Theorem 4.2

Let \(S\) be an excellent scheme of characteristic zero and \(f\colon U\!\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\! S\) a separated morphism of finite type. Let \(F\) be a complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules on \(U\), bounded below in degree and with constructible cohomology, \(n\) an integer and \(T\) a closed subset of \(S\). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. For every scheme \(U_{1}\) étale over \(U\), affine over \(S\), one has, denoting by \(f_{1}\) the structural morphism of \(U_{1}\) and by \(F_{1}\) the restriction of \(F_{1}\) to \(U_{1}\):

    \[ \operatorname{prof}_{T}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}(F_{1}))\geqslant n \]

    (cf. prop. 4.1 on the meaning of this relation).

  2. For every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has:

    \[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\delta_{T}(u), \]

    where one sets (cf. 2.2): \(\delta_{T}(u)=\operatorname{deg\,tr}(k(x)/k(s))+\operatorname{codim}\big(\overline{\{ s\} }\cap T, \overline{\{ s\} } \big)\).

Proof▾

\(1^\circ)\) Let \(t\) be a point of \(T\), \(\overline{S}\) the strict localization of \(S\) at a geometric point above \(t\) and \(S^{\prime}\) the completion of \(\overline{S}\), with closed point \(t^{\prime}\); then \(\overline{S}\) is excellent according to (EGA IV 7.9.5), hence \(S^{\prime}\) is a strictly local complete excellent scheme. Given a scheme \(U\) over \(X\) (resp. an \(S\)-morphism \(f\), resp. etc.), we shall denote by \(U^{\prime}\) (resp. \(f^{\prime}\), resp. etc.) the scheme \(U\times_{S}S^{\prime}\) (resp. the morphism \(f_{(S^{\prime})}\), resp. etc.). We have the cartesian square

\[ \xymatrix{ U^{\prime}\ar[r]^-{h}\ar[d]_-{f^{\prime}} & U\ar[d]^-{f}\\ S^{\prime}\ar[r]^-{g} & S, } \]

in which the morphism \(g\) is regular (EGA IV 7.8.2). Let us show that it suffices to prove that (for every point \(t\in T\)) the following two properties are equivalent:

  • \(\textup{(i)}_{t}\)

    For every scheme \(U_{1}\) étale over \(U\), affine over \(S\), setting \(f_{1}\colon U_{1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), one has

    \[ \operatorname{prof}_{t^{\prime}}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime}))\geqslant n. \]
  • \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\)

    For every point \(u^{\prime}\) of \(U^{\prime}\), one has

    \[ \operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})\geqslant n-\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime}). \]

It suffices to prove the following lemma:

Lemma 4.2.1

One has (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) \(\textup{(i)}_{t}\) for every \(t\in T\) and (ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\) for every \(t\in T\).

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) \(\textup{(i)}_{t}\) for every \(t\in T\). Indeed (i) is equivalent to saying that, for every scheme \(U_{1}\) étale over \(U\), affine over \(S\), one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{T}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}(F_{1}))\geqslant n\;; \]

now according to 1.8

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{T}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}(F_{1}))=\inf_{t\in T}\operatorname{prof}_{t}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}(F_{1})). \]

Since \(g^{\ast}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}(F_{1}))\simeq\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime})\) (SGA 4 XVII), one has according to 1.16

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{t}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}(F_{1}))=\operatorname{prof}_{t^{\prime}}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime})), \]

hence (i) is equivalent to saying that one has, for every \(t\in T\), \(\operatorname{prof}_{t^{\prime}}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime}))\geqslant n\), which is none other than \(\textup{(i)}_{t}\).

(ii)\(_{t}\) for every \(t\in T\) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). Indeed let \(u\in U\); one must show the relation

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\delta_{T}(u), \]

where \(\delta_{T}(u)=\inf_{t\in T\cap\overline{\{ s\} }}\delta_{t}(u)\) (cf. 2.2); one is thus reduced to showing that one has, for every \(t\in T\cap\overline{\{ s\} }\)

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\delta_{t}(u). \]

Let \(u^{\prime}\) be a point of \(U^{\prime}\) such that \(h(u^{\prime})=u\) and \(\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime})=\delta_{t}(u)\) (cf. 2.1.1). Since \(h\) is locally acyclic (SGA 4 XIX 4.1), it follows from 1.16 and from the fact that \(u^{\prime}\) is a generic point of \(U_{u}^{\prime}\) that one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})=\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F). \]

But one has according to \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\) \(\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)=\operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})\geqslant n-\delta_{t}(u)\), which proves (ii).

(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\) for every \(t\). With the notation of 2.2.1, for every point \(u^{\prime}\) of \(U^{\prime}\), one has thanks to 1.16

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})\geqslant\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)+2\dim h_{u^{\prime}}^{-1}(u)\geqslant\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)+\dim h_{u^{\prime}}^{-1}(u). \]

Taking into account 2.2.1 and (ii), one obtains

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})\geqslant n-\delta_{T}(u)+\dim h_{u^{\prime}}^{-1}(u)\geqslant n-\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime}), \]

which is none other than \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\).

\(2^\circ)\) \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) \(\textup{(i)}_{t}\). One immediately reduces to the case where \(F\) is bounded in degree, by truncating \(F\) at a sufficiently high level. One can realize \(S^{\prime}\) as a closed subscheme of a complete regular local scheme, hence excellent; it then follows from (SGA 5 I 3.4.3) that there exists a dualizing complex \(K\) on \(S^{\prime}\) and that \(\operatorname{R}^{!}f^{\prime}(K)=K^{\prime}\) is a dualizing complex on \(U^{\prime}\). We shall choose \(K\) in such a way that one has \(\delta^{K}(t^{\prime})=0\) (for the definition of \(\delta^{K}(t^{\prime})\), cf. 2.3), and shall denote by \(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}\) the dual of \(F^{\prime}\) with respect to \(K^{\prime}\). One can reformulate the hypothesis \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\) in the following way:

Lemma 4.2.2

Let \(u^{\prime}\) be a point of \(U^{\prime}\); then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. One has \(\operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})\geqslant n-\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime})\).

  2. One has \(\big(\underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}) \big)_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}=0\) for \(q>-n-\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime})\) (\(\overline{u}^{\prime}\) geometric point above \(u^{\prime}\)).

Let \(\overline{U}^{\prime}\) be the strict localization of \(U^{\prime}\) at \(\overline{u}^{\prime}\) and \(\overline{F}^{\prime}\) the inverse image of \(F\) under the morphism \(\overline{U}^{\prime}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U^{\prime}\). The relation \(\operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})\geqslant n-\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime})\) is equivalent by definition to the following one:

\[ \tag{*} {\operatorname{H}_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}^{i}(\overline{F}^{\prime})=0\quad\textrm{ for }i>n-\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime}).} \]

Let \(\operatorname{D}\big(\operatorname{H}_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}^{i}(\overline{F}^{\prime})\big)\) be the dual of the abelian group \(\operatorname{H}_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}^{i}(\overline{F}^{\prime})\) with respect to \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\). According to 2.3.2, \(K^{\prime}[-2\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime})]=K^{\prime\prime}\) satisfies \(\delta^{K^{\prime\prime}}(u^{\prime})=0\); since \(F^{\prime}\) has constructible cohomology, one has \(\overline{\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}}=\operatorname{D}(\overline{F}^{\prime})\) and the local duality theorem (SGA 5 I 4.5.3) then shows that one has

\[ \operatorname{D}\big(\operatorname{H}_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}^{i}(\overline{F}^{\prime})\big) \simeq\big(\operatorname{H}^{-i-2\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime})}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime})\big)_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}. \]

Hence (*) is equivalent to the relation

\[ \tag{**} \big(\underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime})\big)_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}=0\quad\text{for }q>-n-\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime}). \]

We are now in a position to prove the theorem. The relation (ii)\(_{t}\) is equivalent to the relation (**). Let \(G^{q}=\underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime})\); the affine Lefschetz theorem (3.1) entails in particular that, for every scheme \(U_{1}\) étale over \(U\), affine over \(S\), one has

\[ \operatorname{H}^{p}(U_{1}^{\prime}, G^{q})=0\quad\textrm{ for }p>\delta(G^{q}), \]

where \(\delta(G^{q})\) is the supremum of the \(\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime})\) for the \(u^{\prime}\) such that one has \(G_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}^{q}\neq 0\); according to (**) one has \(\delta(G^{q})\leqslant -n-q\), hence \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\) entails the relation

\[ \operatorname{H}^{p}(U_{1}^{\prime}, \underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}))=0\quad\textrm{ for }p>-q-n. \]

Taking into account the hypercohomology spectral sequence of the functor "sections over \(U_{1}^{\prime}\)" with respect to the complex \(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}\):

\[ \operatorname{E}_{2}^{pq}=\operatorname{H}^{p}(U_{1}^{\prime}, \underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}))\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{\ast}(U_{1}^{\prime}, \operatorname{D} F^{\prime}), \]

one obtains the relation

\[ \tag{{*}{*}{*}} \operatorname{H}^{i}(U_{1}^{\prime}, \operatorname{D} F^{\prime})=0\quad\textrm{ for }i>-n. \]

Conversely suppose the preceding relation satisfied, for every \(U_{1}\) étale over \(U\), affine over \(S\). Let us apply proposition 3.6 replacing \(S\) there by \(\overline{S}^{\prime}\); the hypotheses of 3.6 concerning \(S\) are satisfied, since, for every scheme \(\overline{U}_{1}\) étale over \(\overline{U}\), affine, one can find a scheme over \(\overline{U}_{1}\) which arises by inverse image from a scheme étale over \(U\), affine over \(S\); as for the hypotheses concerning \(F\), they are satisfied thanks to 2.3.3. One thus has, for every point \(u^{\prime}\) of \(U^{\prime}\) such that \(\big(\underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime})\big)_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}\neq 0\):

\[ \delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime})\leqslant -n-q, \]

which is none other than the relation (**); one has thus proved the equivalence

\[ \text{(ii)$_{t}$~$\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}$~(***)}. \]

We are going to transform the relation ({*}{*}{*}); one has first

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(U_{1}^{\prime}, \operatorname{D} F^{\prime})=\big(\underline{H}^{i}(\operatorname{R} f_{1\ast}^{\prime}(\operatorname{D} F_{1}^{\prime}))\big)_{t^{\prime}}\;; \]

but according to (SGA 5 I 1.12), there exists a canonical isomorphism

\[ \operatorname{R} f_{1\ast}^{\prime}(\operatorname{D} F_{1}^{\prime})\simeq\operatorname{D}\big(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime})\big), \]

where \(\operatorname{D}\big(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime})\big)\) denotes the dual of \(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime})\) with respect to \(K\). One thus sees that \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\) is equivalent to

\[ \big(\underline{H}^{i}(\operatorname{D}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime})))\big)_{t^{\prime}}=0\quad\textrm{ for }i>-n. \]

Applying again the local duality theorem (SGA 5 I 4.5.3), but this time at the point \(t^{\prime}\), one finds that

\[ \big(\underline{H}^{i}(\operatorname{D}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime})))\big)_{t^{\prime}}\simeq\operatorname{D}\big(\operatorname{H}_{t^{\prime}}^{-i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime}))\big), \]

and finally \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\) is equivalent to the relation

\[ \operatorname{H}_{t^{\prime}}^{i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime}))=0\quad\textrm{ for }i<n, \]

that is to say \(\operatorname{prof}_{t^{\prime}}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime}))\geqslant n\), which completes the proof of the theorem.

Q.E.D.
Remark 4.2.3

The reasoning simplifies quite considerably when one supposes that \(S\) admits (at least locally) a dualizing complex (for example is locally embeddable in a regular scheme). This avoids the recourse to a completion (the passage to the case \(S\) strictly local being immediate), to 2.3.3 and to the rather unpleasant technical statement 3.6, which one can then replace by the more agreeable reference 3.7.

Corollary 4.3

Let \(S\) be an excellent scheme of characteristic zero and \(f\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a separated morphism of finite type, such that \(U\) is a union of \(c+1\) open sets, affine over \(S\). Let \(F\) be a complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules, bounded below in degree and with constructible cohomology, \(n\) an integer and \(T\) a closed subset of \(S\). Suppose that, for every point \(u\in U\), one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\delta_{T}(u). \]

Then one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{T}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(F))\geqslant n-c. \]

Indeed let \(U_{i}\), \(0\leqslant i\leqslant c\), be a covering of \(U\) by open sets \(U_{i}\), affine over \(S\). Resuming the notation of the proof of 4.2, one has, for every \(i\),

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(U_{i}^{\prime}, \underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}))=0\quad\textrm{ for }i>-n. \]

Using the spectral sequence that relates the cohomology of \(U\) to that of the covering formed by the \(U_{i}\) (SGA 4 V 2.4), the preceding relation shows that one has

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(U^{\prime}, \underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}))=0\quad\textrm{ for }i>-n+c. \]

The corollary then follows from the end of the proof of 4.2.

Corollary 4.4

Let \(S\) be an excellent scheme of characteristic zero, \(g\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism, \(U\) an open set of \(X\), a union of \(c+1\) open sets affine over \(S\), \(Y\) a closed subscheme with underlying space \(X-U\) and \(j\colon Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the natural morphism. Let \(F\) be a complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules on \(X\), bounded below in degree and with constructible cohomology, \(T\) a closed subset of \(S\) and \(n\) an integer. Suppose that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\delta_{T}(u). \]

Then the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}_{g^{-1}(T)!}^{i}(X/S, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{(g^{-1}(T)\cap Y)!}^{i}(Y/S, j^{\ast}F) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c-1\).

This follows immediately from 4.1 and 4.3.

Corollary 4.5 (Local Lefschetz theorem)

Let \(S\) be an excellent henselian local scheme of characteristic zero, \(t\) the closed point of \(S\), \(X\) a scheme proper over \(S^{\prime}=S-\{ t\}\) and \(U\) an open set of \(X\), a union of \(c+1\) affine open sets. Let \(Y\) be a closed subscheme of \(X\), with underlying space \(X-U\), \(j\colon Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism, \(F\) a complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules on \(X\), bounded below in degree and with constructible cohomology, and \(n\) an integer. Suppose that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u), \quad\text{where } \delta_{t}^{\prime}(u)=\delta_{t}(u)-1. \]

Then the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(Y, j^{\ast}F) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c-1\).

Let \(f\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be the canonical morphism; it follows from 4.2, applied by replacing \(n\) by \(n+1\), that one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{t}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(F|U))\geqslant n+1-c. \]

The preceding relation shows that the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(S, \operatorname{R}_{!}f(F|U))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R}_{!}f(F|U)) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-c\), injective for \(i=n-c\). Since \(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(F|U)\) is zero outside \(S^{\prime}\), one has \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(S, \operatorname{R}_{!}f(F|U))\simeq\big(\underline{H}^{i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(F|U))\big)_{t}=0\), and consequently

\[ \tag{*} \operatorname{H}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R}_{!}f(F|U))=0\quad\text{for }i<n-c. \]

Let \(g\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S^{\prime}\), \(h\colon Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S^{\prime}\), \(f^{\prime}\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S^{\prime}\) be the canonical morphisms. It follows from the distinguished triangle

\[ \xymatrix@C=0pt{ & \mathrm{R} h_{\ast}(j^{\ast}F)\ar[ld] & \\ \mathrm{R}_{!}f^{\prime}(F|U)\ar[rr] & & \mathrm{R} g_{\ast}(F)\ar[lu] } \]

that condition (*) is equivalent to the fact that the morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R} g_{\ast}(F))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R} h_{\ast}(j^{\ast}F)) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c-1\). Since this morphism is canonically identified with the morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(Y, j^{\ast}F), \]

the conclusion follows at once.

Corollary 4.6 (Global Lefschetz theorem)

Let \(S\) be the spectrum of a field, \(X\) a scheme proper over \(S\) and \(U\) an open set of \(X\), a union of \(c+1\) affine open sets. Let \(Y\) be a closed subscheme of \(X\), with underlying space \(X-U\), \(j\colon Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism, \(F\) a complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules on \(X\), bounded below in degree and with constructible cohomology and \(n\) an integer. Suppose that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\dim\big(\overline{\{ u\} }\big). \]

Then the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(Y, j^{\ast}F) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c-1\).

More generally, if \(g\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a separated morphism of finite type, the hypotheses on \(S\), \(U\), \(Y\), \(F\) being the same as before, then the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}_{!}^{i}(X/S, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{!}^{i}(Y/S, j^{\ast}F) \]

(where \(\operatorname{H}_{!}^{i}\) denotes cohomology with proper support, that is to say \(\operatorname{H}_{!}^{i}(X, F)=\operatorname{H}^{i}(S, \operatorname{R}_{!}g(F))\)) is bijective for \(i<n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c-1\).

The corollary is a particular case of 4.4, with \(T=S\).

Here is a partial converse to 4.3:

Proposition 4.7

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(f\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism of finite type. Suppose that there exists a dualizing complex \(K\) on \(S\) and that \(\operatorname{R}^{!}f(K)\) is a dualizing complex on \(U\). Let \(T\) be a closed subset of \(S\) and \(c\) an integer. Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. For every complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules \(F\) on \(U\), bounded below in degree and with constructible cohomology, and for every integer \(n\) such that one has, for every point \(u\) of \(U\),

    \[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\delta_{T}(u), \]

    one has

    \[ \operatorname{prof}_{T}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(F))\geqslant n-c. \]

  2. For every sheaf of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules \(G\) on \(U\), constructible, and for every point \(t\in T\), one has

    \[ \big(\operatorname{R}^{p}f_{\ast}(G)\big)_{\overline{t}}=0\quad\textrm{ for }p>\delta(G, f, t)+c \]

    (let us recall, according to (SGA X 4 XIX 6.0), that \(\delta(G, f, t)=\sup\{ \delta_{t}(u)|t\in\overline{\{ u\} }\textrm{ and }G_{\overline{u}}\neq 0\}\)).

N.B. Condition (ii) is satisfied by virtue of 3.1 if \(f\) is separated and if \(U\) is, locally on \(S\) for the étale topology, a union of \(c+1\) open sets affine over \(S\), hence 4.7 contains 4.31.

One can obviously suppose that \(S\) is local and that \(T\) is the closed point \(t\) of \(S\). The proof of (ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i) is essentially identical to part \(2^\circ)\) of the proof of 4.2. Let us show quickly that (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). The local duality theorem (SGA 5 I 4.3.2) applied to \(\operatorname{D} G\) shows that

\[ \operatorname{D}\big(\operatorname{H}_{\overline{u}}^{i}(\operatorname{D} G)\big) \simeq\big(\underline{H}^{-i-2\delta_{t}(u)}(G)\big)_{\overline{u}}. \]

Since \(G\) is concentrated in degree \(0\), one therefore has \(\operatorname{H}_{\overline{u}}^{i}(\operatorname{D} G)=0\) except perhaps for \(i=-2\delta_{t}(u)\); more precisely

\begin{align*} \operatorname{prof}_{u}(\operatorname{D} G)=-2\delta_{t}(u) &\;\textrm{ if }\; G_{\overline{u}}\neq 0, \\ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(\operatorname{D} G)=\infty &\;\textrm{ if }\; G_{\overline{u}}=0. \end{align*}

It follows that one has, for every \(u\in U\):

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(\operatorname{D} G)\geqslant -n-\delta_{t}(u). \]

It then follows from hypothesis (i) that one has \(\operatorname{prof}_{t}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(\operatorname{D} G))\geqslant -n-c\). One transforms this relation using the isomorphism \(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(\operatorname{D} G)\simeq\operatorname{D}(\operatorname{R} f_{\ast}(G))\) (SGA 5 I 1.12) and applying the local duality theorem at the point \(t\); one thus obtains

\[ \big(\underline{H}^{i}(\operatorname{R} f_{\ast}(G)\big)_{\overline{t}}=0\quad\textrm{ pour }i>n+c, \]

which is none other than (ii).

4.8

The hypotheses being those of 4.4 with \(g\) proper (resp. 4.5, resp. 4.6 with \(g\) proper), if \(V\) is an open neighbourhood of \(Y\) in \(X\), the morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(V, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(Y, j^{\ast}F) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c-1\). If \(i\colon V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is the canonical morphism, it indeed suffices, to see this, to apply 4.4 (resp. 4.5, resp. 4.6) to the complex \(\operatorname{R} i_{\ast}(F{|V})\). One may ask the question whether the preceding morphism is bijective if \(i=n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c\). It evidently suffices that the hypotheses be satisfied when one replaces \(n\) by \(n+1\); the following proposition shows that a little less suffices.

Proposition 4.9

Let \(S\) be an excellent local scheme of characteristic zero, with closed point \(t\) (resp. in addition to the preceding conditions one supposes \(S\) henselian), \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a scheme proper over \(S\) (resp. proper over \(S-\{ s\}\)) and \(U\) an open set of \(X\) a union of \(c+1\) affine open sets. Let \(Y\) be a closed subscheme of \(X\), with underlying space \(X-U\), \(j\colon Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism, \(F\) a complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules on \(X\), bounded below in degree and with constructible cohomology, and \(n\) an integer. One supposes that one has, for every point \(u\) of \(U\),

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant\inf(n-1, n-\delta_{t}(u))\quad(\textrm{resp. }\;\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant\inf(n-1, n+1-\delta_{t}(u))). \]

Then for every open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(Y\) in \(X\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}_{f^{-1}(t)}^{i}(V, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{f^{-1}(t)\cap Y}^{i}(Y, j^{\ast}F)\quad(\textrm{resp. }\;\operatorname{H}^{i}(V, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(Y, j^{\ast}F)) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-c-2\) and injective for \(i=n-c-2\). Moreover, there exists an open neighbourhood \(V_{0}\) of \(Y\) in \(X\), such that, for every other such \(V\) with \(V\subset V_{0}\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}_{f^{-1}(t)\cap V}^{i}(V, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{f^{-1}(t)\cap Y}^{i}(Y, j^{\ast}F)\quad(\textrm{resp. }\;\operatorname{H}^{i}(V, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(Y, j^{\ast}F)) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c-1\).

Proof▾

Let us set, to simplify, \(\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u)=\delta_{t}(u)\) (resp. \(\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u)=\delta_{t}(u)-1\)). One deduces from 4.8 the first assertion of 4.9, since the hypotheses of 4.4 (resp. 4.5) are satisfied when one replaces \(n\) there by \(n-1\). They are also satisfied for \(n\) itself, except at the points \(u\) such that \(\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u)=0\). Now, for a \(u\in U\), to say that one has \(\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u)=0\) is equivalent to saying that \(u\) is a closed point of \(U_{t}\) (resp. a closed point of \(X\)). Let \(E\) be the set of points of \(U\) such that \(\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u)=0\); let us show that, for all the points \(u\in E\), except a finite number, one has \(\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n\). Let \(\overline{S}\) be the strict localization of \(S\) at \(t\), \(S^{\prime}\) the completion of \(\overline{S}\), with closed point \(t^{\prime}\), and consider the cartesian square

\[ \xymatrix{ U^{\prime}\ar[r]^-{h}\ar[d]_-{f^{\prime}} & U\ar[d]^-{f}\\ S^{\prime}\ar[r]^-{g} & S.} \]

The depth hypotheses at the points of \(U\) are preserved when one replaces \(U\) by \(U^{\prime}\) and \(F\) by the inverse image \(F^{\prime}\) of \(F\) on \(U^{\prime}\). Indeed let \(u^{\prime}\in U^{\prime}\) and \(u=h(u^{\prime})\). If \(u\not\in E\), one has the relation \(\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u)\), and it follows from 4.2.1 that this entails the relation \(\operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})\geqslant n-\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u^{\prime})\). If \(u\in E\), \(u^{\prime}\) is a closed point of \(U_{t}^{\prime}\) (resp. a closed point of \(X^{\prime}=X\times_{S}S^{\prime}\)), and, since the fiber \(U_{u}^{\prime}\) of \(h\) at \(u\) is of dimension zero and \(h\) regular, it follows from 1.16 that one has \(\operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})=\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-1\).

Let then \(K\) be a dualizing complex on \(S^{\prime}\), normalized at the closed point \(t^{\prime}\), and \(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}\) the dual of \(F^{\prime}\) with respect to \(\operatorname{R}^{!}f(K)\). According to 4.2.2, the étale depth hypotheses at the points of \(U^{\prime}\) translate into the relations:

\[ \big(\underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime})\big)_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}=0\quad\textrm{ for }q>-n-\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u^{\prime})\quad(\textrm{resp. }q>-n-2-\delta_{t^{\prime}}^{\prime}(u^{\prime})), \]

if \(u^{\prime}\) is not a point of \(E^{\prime}=h^{-1}(E)\),

\[ \big(\underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime})\big)_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}=0\quad\textrm{ for }q>-(n-1)\quad(\textrm{resp. }q>-n-1), \textrm{ if }u^{\prime}\in E^{\prime}. \]

Let \(G=\underline{H}^{-(n-1)}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime})\) (resp. \(G=\underline{H}^{-n-1}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime})\)); since \(G\) is a constructible sheaf, the set of points at which the geometric fiber is nonzero is a constructible set (SGA 4 IX 2.4 (iv)); now by hypothesis this set is contained in the set \(E^{\prime}\) of closed points of \(U_{t^{\prime}}^{\prime}\) (resp. of points of \(U^{\prime}\) closed in \(X^{\prime}\)); it therefore follows from 4.9.1 below that this set is reduced to a finite number of points. Applying 4.2.2, one sees that, for all the points of \(E^{\prime}\), except a finite number, one has \(\operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})\geqslant n\). It indeed follows by 1.16 that, for all the points of \(E^{\prime}\) except a finite number, one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n. \]

Let \(V\) be an open neighbourhood of \(Y\) in \(X\), contained in the complement in \(X\) of the finite set of points \(u\) of \(E\) for which one has \(\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)=n-1\). If \(i\colon V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is the canonical immersion, let

\[ F_{1}=\operatorname{R} i_{\ast}(F{|V})\;; \]

then \(F_{1}\) is a complex of sheaves on \(X\), with constructible cohomology (SGA 4 XIX 5.1) and bounded below in degree. We are going to see that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), the complex \(F_{1}\) satisfies the relation

\[ \tag{*} \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F_{1})\geqslant n-\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u). \]

If \(u\in U\cap V\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F_{1})=\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\), and the relation (*) is satisfied by hypothesis at the points of \(U\) which do not belong to \(E\); for the latter, it is also satisfied according to the choice of \(V\). Finally, if \(u\in U\) and \(u\not\in V\), one has according to 1.6 g) \(\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F_{1})=\infty\). One then applies 4.4 (resp. 4.5) replacing \(F\) by \(F_{1}\); one obtains the announced result, taking into account the fact that one has, for every \(i\):

\[ \operatorname{H}_{f^{-1}(t)}^{i}(X, \operatorname{R} i_{\ast}(F{|V}))\simeq\operatorname{H}_{f^{-1}(t)\cap V}^{i}(V, F)\quad(\textrm{resp. }\operatorname{H}^{i}(X, \operatorname{R} i_{\ast}(F{|V}))\simeq\operatorname{H}^{i}(V, F). \]
Q.E.D.
Lemma 4.9.1

A constructible set \(E\) contained in the set of closed points of a noetherian scheme \(X\) is reduced to a finite number of points.

Indeed, \(E\) is a finite union of sets of the form \(U\cap\complement V\), where \(U\) and \(V\) are open sets of \(X\); by hypothesis all the points of \(U\cap\complement V\) are maximal points of this set, hence they are finite in number.

blocks.html: 4 Main theorem and variants

Let \(g\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a separated morphism of finite type, \(T\) a closed subset of \(S\), \(Z=g^{-1}(T)\) and \(F\) a complex of abelian sheaves on \(X\), bounded below in degree. We call \(i\)-th cohomology group of \(F\), with proper support, with support in \(Z\) the group
\[ \operatorname{H}_{Z!}^{i}(X/S, F)=\operatorname{H}_{T}^{i}(S, \operatorname{R}_{!}g(F)), \]
where \(\operatorname{R}_{!}g\) denotes "the direct image with proper support" (SGA 4 XVII). In the particular case where \(g\) is proper, one simply has
\[ \operatorname{H}_{Z!}^{i}(X/S, F)=\operatorname{H}_{Z}^{i}(X, F). \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.1

Let \(f\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be a morphism of finite type, \(F\) a complex of abelian sheaves on \(U\), bounded below in degree. Suppose that we have a factorization of \(f\):

\[ \xymatrix@C=15pt{ U\ar[rr]^-{i}\ar[rd]_-{f} & & X\ar[ld]^-{g}\\ & S & } \]

where \(i\) is an open immersion and \(g\) a separated morphism of finite type, and let \(G\) denote a complex of abelian sheaves on \(X\), bounded below in degree, which extends \(F\). Let \(Y\) be a closed subscheme of \(X\) with underlying space \(X-U\), so that we have a commutative diagram:

\[ \xymatrix@C=15pt{ Y\ar[rr]^-{j}\ar[rd]_-{h} & & X\ar[ld]^-{g}\\ & S & } \]

Finally, let \(n\) be an integer and \(T\) a closed subset of \(S\). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. One has \(\operatorname{prof}_{T}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(F))\geqslant n\).

  2. The canonical morphism

    \[ \underline{H}_{T}^{i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}g(G))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\underline{H}_{T}^{i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}h(j^{\ast}G)) \]

    is bijective for \(i<n-1\), injective for \(i=n-1\).

  3. For every scheme \(S^{\prime}\) étale over \(S\), if one denotes by \(X^{\prime}\) (resp. \(f^{\prime}\), resp. etc.) the scheme \(X\times_{S}S^{\prime}\) (resp. the morphism \(f_{(S^{\prime})}\), resp. etc.), the canonical morphism

    \[ \operatorname{H}_{g^{\prime -1}(T^{\prime})!}^{i}(X^{\prime}/S^{\prime}, G^{\prime})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{h^{\prime -1}(T^{\prime})!}^{i}(Y^{\prime}/S^{\prime}, j^{\prime\ast}G^{\prime}) \]

    is bijective for \(i<n-1\), injective for \(i=n-1\).
Consider in the derived category \(\operatorname{D}^{+}(X)\) (cf. [3]) the distinguished triangle
\[ \xymatrix@C=15pt{ & j_{\ast}j^{\ast}G\ar[ld] & \\ i_{!}F\ar[rr] & & G.\ar[lu]} \]
Applying the functor \(\operatorname{R}_{!}g\) to this triangle, one obtains the triangle
\[ \tag{*} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix@C=0pt{ & \mathrm{R}_{!}h(j^{\ast}G)\ar[ld] & \\ \mathrm{R}_{!}f(F)\ar[rr] & & \mathrm{R}_{!}g(G).\ar[lu] } \end{array} \]
Let us show that (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (ii). Indeed, according to definition 1.2, (i) is equivalent to the relation
\[ \underline{H}_{T}^{i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(F))=0\quad\textrm{ for }i<n\textrm{;} \]
now from (*) one deduces the exact sequence of sheaves

\[ \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\underline{H}_{T}^{i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(F))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\underline{H}_{T}^{i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}g(G))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\underline{H}_{T}^{i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}h(j^{\ast}G))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}, \]
whence the equivalence of (i) and (ii).

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (iii). Indeed (i) is equivalent to saying that, for every scheme \(S^{\prime}\) étale over \(S\), one has the relation
\[ \tag{**} {\operatorname{H}_{T^{\prime}}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R}_{!}f^{\prime}(F^{\prime}))=0\quad\textrm{ for }i<n.} \]
Now from (*) one deduces the exact sequence of abelian groups

\[ \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{T^{\prime}}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R}_{!}f^{\prime}(F^{\prime}))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{T^{\prime}}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R}_{!}g^{\prime}(G^{\prime}))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_{T^{\prime}}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R}_{!}h^{\prime}(j^{\prime\ast}G^{\prime}))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\;; \]
taking into account 4.0, this exact sequence is written in the form

\[ \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{T^{\prime}}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R}_{!}f^{\prime}(F^{\prime}))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{g^{\prime -1}(T^{\prime})!}^{i}(X^{\prime}/S^{\prime}, G^{\prime})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{h^{\prime -1}(T^{\prime})!}^{i}(Y^{\prime}/S^{\prime}, j^{\prime\ast}G^{\prime}) \]
The equivalence of (i) and (iii) follows from this, taking into account the form (**) of (i).

When \(f\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is affine, we are going to give local conditions on \(F\) for the conditions (i) to (iii) of 4.1 to be satisfied. In what follows, the schemes considered are excellent schemes of characteristic zero, the sheaves are sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules, where \(m\) is a power of a prime number. If one had resolution of singularities in the sense of (SGA 4 XIX), the results stated, as well as their proofs, would still be valid for excellent schemes of equal characteristic, with \(m\) prime to the characteristic.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.2

Let \(S\) be an excellent scheme of characteristic zero and \(f\colon U\!\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\! S\) a separated morphism of finite type. Let \(F\) be a complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules on \(U\), bounded below in degree and with constructible cohomology, \(n\) an integer and \(T\) a closed subset of \(S\). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. For every scheme \(U_{1}\) étale over \(U\), affine over \(S\), one has, denoting by \(f_{1}\) the structural morphism of \(U_{1}\) and by \(F_{1}\) the restriction of \(F_{1}\) to \(U_{1}\):

    \[ \operatorname{prof}_{T}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}(F_{1}))\geqslant n \]

    (cf. prop. 4.1 on the meaning of this relation).

  2. For every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has:

    \[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\delta_{T}(u), \]

    where one sets (cf. 2.2): \(\delta_{T}(u)=\operatorname{deg\,tr}(k(x)/k(s))+\operatorname{codim}\big(\overline{\{ s\} }\cap T, \overline{\{ s\} } \big)\).

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

\(1^\circ)\) Let \(t\) be a point of \(T\), \(\overline{S}\) the strict localization of \(S\) at a geometric point above \(t\) and \(S^{\prime}\) the completion of \(\overline{S}\), with closed point \(t^{\prime}\); then \(\overline{S}\) is excellent according to (EGA IV 7.9.5), hence \(S^{\prime}\) is a strictly local complete excellent scheme. Given a scheme \(U\) over \(X\) (resp. an \(S\)-morphism \(f\), resp. etc.), we shall denote by \(U^{\prime}\) (resp. \(f^{\prime}\), resp. etc.) the scheme \(U\times_{S}S^{\prime}\) (resp. the morphism \(f_{(S^{\prime})}\), resp. etc.). We have the cartesian square

\[ \xymatrix{ U^{\prime}\ar[r]^-{h}\ar[d]_-{f^{\prime}} & U\ar[d]^-{f}\\ S^{\prime}\ar[r]^-{g} & S, } \]

in which the morphism \(g\) is regular (EGA IV 7.8.2). Let us show that it suffices to prove that (for every point \(t\in T\)) the following two properties are equivalent:

  • \(\textup{(i)}_{t}\)

    For every scheme \(U_{1}\) étale over \(U\), affine over \(S\), setting \(f_{1}\colon U_{1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), one has

    \[ \operatorname{prof}_{t^{\prime}}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime}))\geqslant n. \]
  • \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\)

    For every point \(u^{\prime}\) of \(U^{\prime}\), one has

    \[ \operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})\geqslant n-\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime}). \]

It suffices to prove the following lemma:

Lemma 4.2.1

One has (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) \(\textup{(i)}_{t}\) for every \(t\in T\) and (ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\) for every \(t\in T\).

(i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) \(\textup{(i)}_{t}\) for every \(t\in T\). Indeed (i) is equivalent to saying that, for every scheme \(U_{1}\) étale over \(U\), affine over \(S\), one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{T}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}(F_{1}))\geqslant n\;; \]

now according to 1.8

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{T}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}(F_{1}))=\inf_{t\in T}\operatorname{prof}_{t}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}(F_{1})). \]

Since \(g^{\ast}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}(F_{1}))\simeq\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime})\) (SGA 4 XVII), one has according to 1.16

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{t}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}(F_{1}))=\operatorname{prof}_{t^{\prime}}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime})), \]

hence (i) is equivalent to saying that one has, for every \(t\in T\), \(\operatorname{prof}_{t^{\prime}}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime}))\geqslant n\), which is none other than \(\textup{(i)}_{t}\).

(ii)\(_{t}\) for every \(t\in T\) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). Indeed let \(u\in U\); one must show the relation

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\delta_{T}(u), \]

where \(\delta_{T}(u)=\inf_{t\in T\cap\overline{\{ s\} }}\delta_{t}(u)\) (cf. 2.2); one is thus reduced to showing that one has, for every \(t\in T\cap\overline{\{ s\} }\)

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\delta_{t}(u). \]

Let \(u^{\prime}\) be a point of \(U^{\prime}\) such that \(h(u^{\prime})=u\) and \(\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime})=\delta_{t}(u)\) (cf. 2.1.1). Since \(h\) is locally acyclic (SGA 4 XIX 4.1), it follows from 1.16 and from the fact that \(u^{\prime}\) is a generic point of \(U_{u}^{\prime}\) that one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})=\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F). \]

But one has according to \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\) \(\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)=\operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})\geqslant n-\delta_{t}(u)\), which proves (ii).

(ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\) for every \(t\). With the notation of 2.2.1, for every point \(u^{\prime}\) of \(U^{\prime}\), one has thanks to 1.16

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})\geqslant\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)+2\dim h_{u^{\prime}}^{-1}(u)\geqslant\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)+\dim h_{u^{\prime}}^{-1}(u). \]

Taking into account 2.2.1 and (ii), one obtains

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})\geqslant n-\delta_{T}(u)+\dim h_{u^{\prime}}^{-1}(u)\geqslant n-\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime}), \]

which is none other than \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\).

\(2^\circ)\) \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) \(\textup{(i)}_{t}\). One immediately reduces to the case where \(F\) is bounded in degree, by truncating \(F\) at a sufficiently high level. One can realize \(S^{\prime}\) as a closed subscheme of a complete regular local scheme, hence excellent; it then follows from (SGA 5 I 3.4.3) that there exists a dualizing complex \(K\) on \(S^{\prime}\) and that \(\operatorname{R}^{!}f^{\prime}(K)=K^{\prime}\) is a dualizing complex on \(U^{\prime}\). We shall choose \(K\) in such a way that one has \(\delta^{K}(t^{\prime})=0\) (for the definition of \(\delta^{K}(t^{\prime})\), cf. 2.3), and shall denote by \(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}\) the dual of \(F^{\prime}\) with respect to \(K^{\prime}\). One can reformulate the hypothesis \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\) in the following way:

Lemma 4.2.2

Let \(u^{\prime}\) be a point of \(U^{\prime}\); then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. One has \(\operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})\geqslant n-\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime})\).

  2. One has \(\big(\underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}) \big)_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}=0\) for \(q>-n-\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime})\) (\(\overline{u}^{\prime}\) geometric point above \(u^{\prime}\)).

Let \(\overline{U}^{\prime}\) be the strict localization of \(U^{\prime}\) at \(\overline{u}^{\prime}\) and \(\overline{F}^{\prime}\) the inverse image of \(F\) under the morphism \(\overline{U}^{\prime}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U^{\prime}\). The relation \(\operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})\geqslant n-\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime})\) is equivalent by definition to the following one:

\[ \tag{*} {\operatorname{H}_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}^{i}(\overline{F}^{\prime})=0\quad\textrm{ for }i>n-\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime}).} \]

Let \(\operatorname{D}\big(\operatorname{H}_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}^{i}(\overline{F}^{\prime})\big)\) be the dual of the abelian group \(\operatorname{H}_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}^{i}(\overline{F}^{\prime})\) with respect to \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\). According to 2.3.2, \(K^{\prime}[-2\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime})]=K^{\prime\prime}\) satisfies \(\delta^{K^{\prime\prime}}(u^{\prime})=0\); since \(F^{\prime}\) has constructible cohomology, one has \(\overline{\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}}=\operatorname{D}(\overline{F}^{\prime})\) and the local duality theorem (SGA 5 I 4.5.3) then shows that one has

\[ \operatorname{D}\big(\operatorname{H}_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}^{i}(\overline{F}^{\prime})\big) \simeq\big(\operatorname{H}^{-i-2\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime})}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime})\big)_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}. \]

Hence (*) is equivalent to the relation

\[ \tag{**} \big(\underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime})\big)_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}=0\quad\text{for }q>-n-\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime}). \]

We are now in a position to prove the theorem. The relation (ii)\(_{t}\) is equivalent to the relation (**). Let \(G^{q}=\underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime})\); the affine Lefschetz theorem (3.1) entails in particular that, for every scheme \(U_{1}\) étale over \(U\), affine over \(S\), one has

\[ \operatorname{H}^{p}(U_{1}^{\prime}, G^{q})=0\quad\textrm{ for }p>\delta(G^{q}), \]

where \(\delta(G^{q})\) is the supremum of the \(\delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime})\) for the \(u^{\prime}\) such that one has \(G_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}^{q}\neq 0\); according to (**) one has \(\delta(G^{q})\leqslant -n-q\), hence \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\) entails the relation

\[ \operatorname{H}^{p}(U_{1}^{\prime}, \underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}))=0\quad\textrm{ for }p>-q-n. \]

Taking into account the hypercohomology spectral sequence of the functor "sections over \(U_{1}^{\prime}\)" with respect to the complex \(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}\):

\[ \operatorname{E}_{2}^{pq}=\operatorname{H}^{p}(U_{1}^{\prime}, \underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}))\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{\ast}(U_{1}^{\prime}, \operatorname{D} F^{\prime}), \]

one obtains the relation

\[ \tag{{*}{*}{*}} \operatorname{H}^{i}(U_{1}^{\prime}, \operatorname{D} F^{\prime})=0\quad\textrm{ for }i>-n. \]

Conversely suppose the preceding relation satisfied, for every \(U_{1}\) étale over \(U\), affine over \(S\). Let us apply proposition 3.6 replacing \(S\) there by \(\overline{S}^{\prime}\); the hypotheses of 3.6 concerning \(S\) are satisfied, since, for every scheme \(\overline{U}_{1}\) étale over \(\overline{U}\), affine, one can find a scheme over \(\overline{U}_{1}\) which arises by inverse image from a scheme étale over \(U\), affine over \(S\); as for the hypotheses concerning \(F\), they are satisfied thanks to 2.3.3. One thus has, for every point \(u^{\prime}\) of \(U^{\prime}\) such that \(\big(\underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime})\big)_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}\neq 0\):

\[ \delta_{t^{\prime}}(u^{\prime})\leqslant -n-q, \]

which is none other than the relation (**); one has thus proved the equivalence

\[ \text{(ii)$_{t}$~$\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}$~(***)}. \]

We are going to transform the relation ({*}{*}{*}); one has first

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(U_{1}^{\prime}, \operatorname{D} F^{\prime})=\big(\underline{H}^{i}(\operatorname{R} f_{1\ast}^{\prime}(\operatorname{D} F_{1}^{\prime}))\big)_{t^{\prime}}\;; \]

but according to (SGA 5 I 1.12), there exists a canonical isomorphism

\[ \operatorname{R} f_{1\ast}^{\prime}(\operatorname{D} F_{1}^{\prime})\simeq\operatorname{D}\big(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime})\big), \]

where \(\operatorname{D}\big(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime})\big)\) denotes the dual of \(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime})\) with respect to \(K\). One thus sees that \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\) is equivalent to

\[ \big(\underline{H}^{i}(\operatorname{D}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime})))\big)_{t^{\prime}}=0\quad\textrm{ for }i>-n. \]

Applying again the local duality theorem (SGA 5 I 4.5.3), but this time at the point \(t^{\prime}\), one finds that

\[ \big(\underline{H}^{i}(\operatorname{D}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime})))\big)_{t^{\prime}}\simeq\operatorname{D}\big(\operatorname{H}_{t^{\prime}}^{-i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime}))\big), \]

and finally \(\textup{(ii)}_{t}\) is equivalent to the relation
