\[ \operatorname{H}_{t^{\prime}}^{i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime}))=0\quad\textrm{ for }i<n, \]

that is to say \(\operatorname{prof}_{t^{\prime}}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f_{1}^{\prime}(F_{1}^{\prime}))\geqslant n\), which completes the proof of the theorem.

Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.2.3

The reasoning simplifies quite considerably when one supposes that \(S\) admits (at least locally) a dualizing complex (for example is locally embeddable in a regular scheme). This avoids the recourse to a completion (the passage to the case \(S\) strictly local being immediate), to 2.3.3 and to the rather unpleasant technical statement 3.6, which one can then replace by the more agreeable reference 3.7.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3

Let \(S\) be an excellent scheme of characteristic zero and \(f\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a separated morphism of finite type, such that \(U\) is a union of \(c+1\) open sets, affine over \(S\). Let \(F\) be a complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules, bounded below in degree and with constructible cohomology, \(n\) an integer and \(T\) a closed subset of \(S\). Suppose that, for every point \(u\in U\), one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\delta_{T}(u). \]

Then one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{T}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(F))\geqslant n-c. \]
Indeed let \(U_{i}\), \(0\leqslant i\leqslant c\), be a covering of \(U\) by open sets \(U_{i}\), affine over \(S\). Resuming the notation of the proof of 4.2, one has, for every \(i\),
\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(U_{i}^{\prime}, \underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}))=0\quad\textrm{ for }i>-n. \]
Using the spectral sequence that relates the cohomology of \(U\) to that of the covering formed by the \(U_{i}\) (SGA 4 V 2.4), the preceding relation shows that one has
\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(U^{\prime}, \underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}))=0\quad\textrm{ for }i>-n+c. \]
The corollary then follows from the end of the proof of 4.2.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.4

Let \(S\) be an excellent scheme of characteristic zero, \(g\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism, \(U\) an open set of \(X\), a union of \(c+1\) open sets affine over \(S\), \(Y\) a closed subscheme with underlying space \(X-U\) and \(j\colon Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the natural morphism. Let \(F\) be a complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules on \(X\), bounded below in degree and with constructible cohomology, \(T\) a closed subset of \(S\) and \(n\) an integer. Suppose that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\delta_{T}(u). \]

Then the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}_{g^{-1}(T)!}^{i}(X/S, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{(g^{-1}(T)\cap Y)!}^{i}(Y/S, j^{\ast}F) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c-1\).
This follows immediately from 4.1 and 4.3.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.5 (Local Lefschetz theorem)

Let \(S\) be an excellent henselian local scheme of characteristic zero, \(t\) the closed point of \(S\), \(X\) a scheme proper over \(S^{\prime}=S-\{ t\}\) and \(U\) an open set of \(X\), a union of \(c+1\) affine open sets. Let \(Y\) be a closed subscheme of \(X\), with underlying space \(X-U\), \(j\colon Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism, \(F\) a complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules on \(X\), bounded below in degree and with constructible cohomology, and \(n\) an integer. Suppose that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u), \quad\text{where } \delta_{t}^{\prime}(u)=\delta_{t}(u)-1. \]

Then the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(Y, j^{\ast}F) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c-1\).
Let \(f\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be the canonical morphism; it follows from 4.2, applied by replacing \(n\) by \(n+1\), that one has
\[ \operatorname{prof}_{t}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(F|U))\geqslant n+1-c. \]
The preceding relation shows that the canonical morphism
\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(S, \operatorname{R}_{!}f(F|U))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R}_{!}f(F|U)) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-c\), injective for \(i=n-c\). Since \(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(F|U)\) is zero outside \(S^{\prime}\), one has \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(S, \operatorname{R}_{!}f(F|U))\simeq\big(\underline{H}^{i}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(F|U))\big)_{t}=0\), and consequently
\[ \tag{*} \operatorname{H}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R}_{!}f(F|U))=0\quad\text{for }i<n-c. \]

Let \(g\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S^{\prime}\), \(h\colon Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S^{\prime}\), \(f^{\prime}\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S^{\prime}\) be the canonical morphisms. It follows from the distinguished triangle
\[ \xymatrix@C=0pt{ & \mathrm{R} h_{\ast}(j^{\ast}F)\ar[ld] & \\ \mathrm{R}_{!}f^{\prime}(F|U)\ar[rr] & & \mathrm{R} g_{\ast}(F)\ar[lu] } \]
that condition (*) is equivalent to the fact that the morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R} g_{\ast}(F))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(S^{\prime}, \operatorname{R} h_{\ast}(j^{\ast}F)) \]
is bijective for \(i<n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c-1\). Since this morphism is canonically identified with the morphism
\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(Y, j^{\ast}F), \]
the conclusion follows at once.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6 (Global Lefschetz theorem)

Let \(S\) be the spectrum of a field, \(X\) a scheme proper over \(S\) and \(U\) an open set of \(X\), a union of \(c+1\) affine open sets. Let \(Y\) be a closed subscheme of \(X\), with underlying space \(X-U\), \(j\colon Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism, \(F\) a complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules on \(X\), bounded below in degree and with constructible cohomology and \(n\) an integer. Suppose that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\dim\big(\overline{\{ u\} }\big). \]

Then the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(Y, j^{\ast}F) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c-1\).

More generally, if \(g\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) is a separated morphism of finite type, the hypotheses on \(S\), \(U\), \(Y\), \(F\) being the same as before, then the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}_{!}^{i}(X/S, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{!}^{i}(Y/S, j^{\ast}F) \]

(where \(\operatorname{H}_{!}^{i}\) denotes cohomology with proper support, that is to say \(\operatorname{H}_{!}^{i}(X, F)=\operatorname{H}^{i}(S, \operatorname{R}_{!}g(F))\)) is bijective for \(i<n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c-1\).
The corollary is a particular case of 4.4, with \(T=S\).
Here is a partial converse to 4.3:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.7

Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(f\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a morphism of finite type. Suppose that there exists a dualizing complex \(K\) on \(S\) and that \(\operatorname{R}^{!}f(K)\) is a dualizing complex on \(U\). Let \(T\) be a closed subset of \(S\) and \(c\) an integer. Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. For every complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules \(F\) on \(U\), bounded below in degree and with constructible cohomology, and for every integer \(n\) such that one has, for every point \(u\) of \(U\),

    \[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\delta_{T}(u), \]

    one has

    \[ \operatorname{prof}_{T}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(F))\geqslant n-c. \]

  2. For every sheaf of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules \(G\) on \(U\), constructible, and for every point \(t\in T\), one has

    \[ \big(\operatorname{R}^{p}f_{\ast}(G)\big)_{\overline{t}}=0\quad\textrm{ for }p>\delta(G, f, t)+c \]

    (let us recall, according to (SGA X 4 XIX 6.0), that \(\delta(G, f, t)=\sup\{ \delta_{t}(u)|t\in\overline{\{ u\} }\textrm{ and }G_{\overline{u}}\neq 0\}\)).

N.B. Condition (ii) is satisfied by virtue of 3.1 if \(f\) is separated and if \(U\) is, locally on \(S\) for the étale topology, a union of \(c+1\) open sets affine over \(S\), hence 4.7 contains 4.31.

One can obviously suppose that \(S\) is local and that \(T\) is the closed point \(t\) of \(S\). The proof of (ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i) is essentially identical to part \(2^\circ)\) of the proof of 4.2. Let us show quickly that (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). The local duality theorem (SGA 5 I 4.3.2) applied to \(\operatorname{D} G\) shows that
\[ \operatorname{D}\big(\operatorname{H}_{\overline{u}}^{i}(\operatorname{D} G)\big) \simeq\big(\underline{H}^{-i-2\delta_{t}(u)}(G)\big)_{\overline{u}}. \]
Since \(G\) is concentrated in degree \(0\), one therefore has \(\operatorname{H}_{\overline{u}}^{i}(\operatorname{D} G)=0\) except perhaps for \(i=-2\delta_{t}(u)\); more precisely

\begin{align*} \operatorname{prof}_{u}(\operatorname{D} G)=-2\delta_{t}(u) &\;\textrm{ if }\; G_{\overline{u}}\neq 0, \\ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(\operatorname{D} G)=\infty &\;\textrm{ if }\; G_{\overline{u}}=0. \end{align*}
It follows that one has, for every \(u\in U\):
\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(\operatorname{D} G)\geqslant -n-\delta_{t}(u). \]

It then follows from hypothesis (i) that one has \(\operatorname{prof}_{t}(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(\operatorname{D} G))\geqslant -n-c\). One transforms this relation using the isomorphism \(\operatorname{R}_{!}f(\operatorname{D} G)\simeq\operatorname{D}(\operatorname{R} f_{\ast}(G))\) (SGA 5 I 1.12) and applying the local duality theorem at the point \(t\); one thus obtains
\[ \big(\underline{H}^{i}(\operatorname{R} f_{\ast}(G)\big)_{\overline{t}}=0\quad\textrm{ pour }i>n+c, \]
which is none other than (ii).
The hypotheses being those of 4.4 with \(g\) proper (resp. 4.5, resp. 4.6 with \(g\) proper), if \(V\) is an open neighbourhood of \(Y\) in \(X\), the morphism
\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(V, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(Y, j^{\ast}F) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c-1\). If \(i\colon V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is the canonical morphism, it indeed suffices, to see this, to apply 4.4 (resp. 4.5, resp. 4.6) to the complex \(\operatorname{R} i_{\ast}(F{|V})\). One may ask the question whether the preceding morphism is bijective if \(i=n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c\). It evidently suffices that the hypotheses be satisfied when one replaces \(n\) by \(n+1\); the following proposition shows that a little less suffices.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.9

Let \(S\) be an excellent local scheme of characteristic zero, with closed point \(t\) (resp. in addition to the preceding conditions one supposes \(S\) henselian), \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a scheme proper over \(S\) (resp. proper over \(S-\{ s\}\)) and \(U\) an open set of \(X\) a union of \(c+1\) affine open sets. Let \(Y\) be a closed subscheme of \(X\), with underlying space \(X-U\), \(j\colon Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the canonical morphism, \(F\) a complex of sheaves of \(\mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}\)-modules on \(X\), bounded below in degree and with constructible cohomology, and \(n\) an integer. One supposes that one has, for every point \(u\) of \(U\),

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant\inf(n-1, n-\delta_{t}(u))\quad(\textrm{resp. }\;\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant\inf(n-1, n+1-\delta_{t}(u))). \]

Then for every open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(Y\) in \(X\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}_{f^{-1}(t)}^{i}(V, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{f^{-1}(t)\cap Y}^{i}(Y, j^{\ast}F)\quad(\textrm{resp. }\;\operatorname{H}^{i}(V, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(Y, j^{\ast}F)) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-c-2\) and injective for \(i=n-c-2\). Moreover, there exists an open neighbourhood \(V_{0}\) of \(Y\) in \(X\), such that, for every other such \(V\) with \(V\subset V_{0}\), the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}_{f^{-1}(t)\cap V}^{i}(V, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{f^{-1}(t)\cap Y}^{i}(Y, j^{\ast}F)\quad(\textrm{resp. }\;\operatorname{H}^{i}(V, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(Y, j^{\ast}F)) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c-1\).

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

Let us set, to simplify, \(\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u)=\delta_{t}(u)\) (resp. \(\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u)=\delta_{t}(u)-1\)). One deduces from 4.8 the first assertion of 4.9, since the hypotheses of 4.4 (resp. 4.5) are satisfied when one replaces \(n\) there by \(n-1\). They are also satisfied for \(n\) itself, except at the points \(u\) such that \(\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u)=0\). Now, for a \(u\in U\), to say that one has \(\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u)=0\) is equivalent to saying that \(u\) is a closed point of \(U_{t}\) (resp. a closed point of \(X\)). Let \(E\) be the set of points of \(U\) such that \(\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u)=0\); let us show that, for all the points \(u\in E\), except a finite number, one has \(\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n\). Let \(\overline{S}\) be the strict localization of \(S\) at \(t\), \(S^{\prime}\) the completion of \(\overline{S}\), with closed point \(t^{\prime}\), and consider the cartesian square

\[ \xymatrix{ U^{\prime}\ar[r]^-{h}\ar[d]_-{f^{\prime}} & U\ar[d]^-{f}\\ S^{\prime}\ar[r]^-{g} & S.} \]

The depth hypotheses at the points of \(U\) are preserved when one replaces \(U\) by \(U^{\prime}\) and \(F\) by the inverse image \(F^{\prime}\) of \(F\) on \(U^{\prime}\). Indeed let \(u^{\prime}\in U^{\prime}\) and \(u=h(u^{\prime})\). If \(u\not\in E\), one has the relation \(\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u)\), and it follows from 4.2.1 that this entails the relation \(\operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})\geqslant n-\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u^{\prime})\). If \(u\in E\), \(u^{\prime}\) is a closed point of \(U_{t}^{\prime}\) (resp. a closed point of \(X^{\prime}=X\times_{S}S^{\prime}\)), and, since the fiber \(U_{u}^{\prime}\) of \(h\) at \(u\) is of dimension zero and \(h\) regular, it follows from 1.16 that one has \(\operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})=\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n-1\).

Let then \(K\) be a dualizing complex on \(S^{\prime}\), normalized at the closed point \(t^{\prime}\), and \(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime}\) the dual of \(F^{\prime}\) with respect to \(\operatorname{R}^{!}f(K)\). According to 4.2.2, the étale depth hypotheses at the points of \(U^{\prime}\) translate into the relations:

\[ \big(\underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime})\big)_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}=0\quad\textrm{ for }q>-n-\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u^{\prime})\quad(\textrm{resp. }q>-n-2-\delta_{t^{\prime}}^{\prime}(u^{\prime})), \]

if \(u^{\prime}\) is not a point of \(E^{\prime}=h^{-1}(E)\),

\[ \big(\underline{H}^{q}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime})\big)_{\overline{u}^{\prime}}=0\quad\textrm{ for }q>-(n-1)\quad(\textrm{resp. }q>-n-1), \textrm{ if }u^{\prime}\in E^{\prime}. \]

Let \(G=\underline{H}^{-(n-1)}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime})\) (resp. \(G=\underline{H}^{-n-1}(\operatorname{D} F^{\prime})\)); since \(G\) is a constructible sheaf, the set of points at which the geometric fiber is nonzero is a constructible set (SGA 4 IX 2.4 (iv)); now by hypothesis this set is contained in the set \(E^{\prime}\) of closed points of \(U_{t^{\prime}}^{\prime}\) (resp. of points of \(U^{\prime}\) closed in \(X^{\prime}\)); it therefore follows from 4.9.1 below that this set is reduced to a finite number of points. Applying 4.2.2, one sees that, for all the points of \(E^{\prime}\), except a finite number, one has \(\operatorname{prof}_{u^{\prime}}(F^{\prime})\geqslant n\). It indeed follows by 1.16 that, for all the points of \(E^{\prime}\) except a finite number, one has

\[ \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\geqslant n. \]

Let \(V\) be an open neighbourhood of \(Y\) in \(X\), contained in the complement in \(X\) of the finite set of points \(u\) of \(E\) for which one has \(\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)=n-1\). If \(i\colon V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is the canonical immersion, let

\[ F_{1}=\operatorname{R} i_{\ast}(F{|V})\;; \]

then \(F_{1}\) is a complex of sheaves on \(X\), with constructible cohomology (SGA 4 XIX 5.1) and bounded below in degree. We are going to see that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), the complex \(F_{1}\) satisfies the relation

\[ \tag{*} \operatorname{prof}_{u}(F_{1})\geqslant n-\delta_{t}^{\prime}(u). \]

If \(u\in U\cap V\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F_{1})=\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F)\), and the relation (*) is satisfied by hypothesis at the points of \(U\) which do not belong to \(E\); for the latter, it is also satisfied according to the choice of \(V\). Finally, if \(u\in U\) and \(u\not\in V\), one has according to 1.6 g) \(\operatorname{prof}_{u}(F_{1})=\infty\). One then applies 4.4 (resp. 4.5) replacing \(F\) by \(F_{1}\); one obtains the announced result, taking into account the fact that one has, for every \(i\):

\[ \operatorname{H}_{f^{-1}(t)}^{i}(X, \operatorname{R} i_{\ast}(F{|V}))\simeq\operatorname{H}_{f^{-1}(t)\cap V}^{i}(V, F)\quad(\textrm{resp. }\operatorname{H}^{i}(X, \operatorname{R} i_{\ast}(F{|V}))\simeq\operatorname{H}^{i}(V, F). \]
Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.9.1

A constructible set \(E\) contained in the set of closed points of a noetherian scheme \(X\) is reduced to a finite number of points.

Indeed, \(E\) is a finite union of sets of the form \(U\cap\complement V\), where \(U\) and \(V\) are open sets of \(X\); by hypothesis all the points of \(U\cap\complement V\) are maximal points of this set, hence they are finite in number.

footnotes.html: At least in the case where \(S\) admits locally a dualizing complex, e.g. \(S\) locally embeddable in a regular scheme.

pages.title: Geometric depth
pages.html:
5 Geometric depth

To apply 4.2 and its corollaries in practice, one must have a convenient criterion allowing one to verify the étale depth hypotheses at the points of \(U\). We shall give such a criterion, using the local Lefschetz theorem (4.5).

5.1

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring; when we speak of the étale depth of \(A\), we shall mean the depth at the closed point. We shall introduce a notion of "geometric depth of \(A\)", and use 4.5 to compare it with the étale depth \(\operatorname{prof\;\acute{e}t}(A)\).

Proposition 5.2

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring; suppose that \(A\) is isomorphic to a quotient of a regular local ring \(B\) by an ideal \(I\) (this holds for example when \(A\) is complete, by virtue of Cohen's theorem (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{IV}}\) 19.8.8)). Let \(q\) be the minimal number of generators of \(I\); then the number \(\dim(B)-q\) is independent of the choice of \(B\).

The minimal number of generators of \(I\) is also equal to the rank of the \(k\)-vector space \(I\otimes_{B}k\), where \(k\) denotes the residue field of \(A\). One reduces at once to the case where \(A\) is complete, for one has \(\widehat{A}\simeq\widehat{B}/\widehat{I}\) with \(\dim \widehat{B}=\dim B\) and \(\operatorname{rg}_{k}(I\otimes_{B}k)=\operatorname{rg}_{k}(\widehat{I}\otimes_{\widehat{B}}k)\); for the same reason one may suppose that the rings \(B\) are complete. Let \(B\) and \(B^{\prime}\) be two complete regular local rings, \(f\colon B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\), \(f^{\prime}\colon B^{\prime}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) two surjective homomorphisms and \(I=\operatorname{Ker}(f)\), \(I^{\prime}=\operatorname{Ker}(f^{\prime})\). One must show that

\[ \dim B-\operatorname{rg}_{k}(I\otimes_{B}k)=\dim B^{\prime}-\operatorname{rg}_{k}(I^{\prime}\otimes_{B^{\prime}}k). \]

Let us first place ourselves in the case where one has a factorization of the form

\[ \xymatrix@C=15pt{ B\ar[rr]^-{f}\ar[rd]_-{g} & & A\\ & B^{\prime}\ar[ru]_-{f^{\prime}} & } \]

with \(g\) surjective. Let \(J=\operatorname{Ker}(g)\); then \(J\subset I\) and \(I/J=I^{\prime}\). Since \(B^{\prime}\) is regular, \(\dim(B^{\prime})=\dim(B)-\operatorname{rg}_{k}(J\otimes_{B}k)\) and \(J\) is generated by elements forming part of a regular system of parameters of \(B\). It follows that one has the exact sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} J\otimes_{B}k\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} I\otimes_{B}k\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} J/I\otimes_{B^{\prime}}k\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

and consequently

\[ \dim B-\operatorname{rg}_{k}(I\otimes_{B}k)=\dim B-\operatorname{rg}_{k}(J\otimes_{B}k)-\operatorname{rg}_{k}(J/I\otimes_{B^{\prime}}k)=\dim B^{\prime}-\operatorname{rg}_{k}(I^{\prime}\otimes_{B^{\prime}}k). \]

The general case reduces to the preceding one; to see this, it suffices to show that one can find a complete regular local ring \(B^{\prime\prime}\) and surjective homomorphisms \(g\colon B^{\prime\prime}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) and \(g^{\prime}\colon B^{\prime\prime}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B^{\prime}\), making the diagram commutative

\[ \tag{*} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix@C=10pt@R=15pt{ & B\ar[rd]^-{f} & \\ B^{\prime\prime}\ar[ru]^-{g}\ar[rd]_-{g^{\prime}} & & A\\ & B^{\prime}.\ar[ru]_-{f^{\prime}} & } \end{array} \]

Now, if \(W\) is a Cohen ring with residue field \(k\), one has a local morphism \(W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) which lifts to \(B\) and \(B^{\prime}\) (EGA IV 19.8.6), so that one has the commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix@R=15pt@C=10pt{ & B\ar[rd] & \\ W\ar[ru]\ar[rd] & & A\\ & B^{\prime}.\ar[ru] & } \]

One can find integers \(n\) and \(n^{\prime}\) and surjective morphisms \(h\colon W[\![ T_{1},\dots, T_{n}]\!]\!\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\! B\) and \(h^{\prime}\colon W[\![ T_{1}^{\prime},\dots, T_{n^{\prime}}^{\prime}]\!]\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B^{\prime}\) which are morphisms of \(W\)-algebras (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{IV}}\) 19.8.8); if one then sets \(B^{\prime\prime}=W[\![ T_{1},\dots, T_{n}, T_{1}^{\prime},\dots, T_{n^{\prime}}^{\prime}]\!]\) and if one defines \(g\) and \(g^{\prime}\) as morphisms of \(W\)-algebras such that

\[ g(T_{i})=h(T_{i}), \quad g(T_{i}^{\prime})=b_{i}, \quad g^{\prime}(T_{i})=b_{i}^{\prime}, \quad g^{\prime}(T_{i}^{\prime})=h^{\prime}(T_{i}^{\prime}), \]

where \(b_{i}\) (resp. \(b_{i}^{\prime}\)) is an element of \(B\) (resp. of \(B^{\prime}\)) which lifts \(h^{\prime}.f^{\prime}(T_{i}^{\prime})\) (resp. \(h.f(T_{i})\)), the diagram (*) is indeed commutative.

Proposition 5.2 justifies the following definition:

Definition 5.3

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring, \(\widehat{A}\) its completion, which is therefore isomorphic to the quotient of a complete regular local ring \(B\) by an ideal \(I\); if \(q\) is the minimal number of generators of \(I\), one calls geometric depth of \(A\) the number

\[ \operatorname{prof\;g\acute{e}om}(A)=\dim B-q. \]
Proposition 5.4

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring. Then one has

\[ \operatorname{prof\;g\acute{e}om}(A)\leqslant\dim A, \]

and one has equality if and only if \(A\) is a complete intersection.

One may suppose \(A\) complete. Let then \(A=B/I\), where \(B\) is a complete regular local ring and \(I\) an ideal of \(B\). If \((x_{1},\dots, x_{q})\) is a minimal system of generators of \(I\), one has \(\dim A\geqslant\dim B-q\), and to say that \(\dim A=\dim B-q\) is equivalent to saying that \((x_{1},\dots, x_{q})\) forms part of a system of parameters of \(B\) (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{IV}}\) 16.3.7); the proposition follows immediately.

Proposition 5.5

Let \(A\) and \(A^{\prime}\) be noetherian local rings, \(f\colon A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A^{\prime}\) a local homomorphism. Suppose that \(f\) is flat and that, denoting by \(k\) the residue field of \(A\), \(A^{\prime}\otimes_{A}k\) is a field, a separable extension of \(K\). Then one has

\[ \operatorname{prof\;g\acute{e}om}(A)=\operatorname{prof\;g\acute{e}om}(A^{\prime}). \]

Up to replacing \(A\) and \(A^{\prime}\) by their completions, one may suppose \(A\) and \(A^{\prime}\) complete (it follows from (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{III}}\) 10.2.1) that the flatness hypothesis is preserved, and this is evident for the other hypotheses). Let then \(A=B/I\), where \(B\) is a regular local ring and \(I\) an ideal of \(B\). Since \(A^{\prime}\) is formally smooth over \(A\) (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{IV}}\) 19.8.2), it follows from (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{IV}}\) 19.7.2) that one can find a complete noetherian local ring \(B^{\prime}\) and a local homomorphism \(B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B^{\prime}\), such that \(B^{\prime}\) is a flat \(B\)-module and such that one has \(B^{\prime}\otimes_{B}A\simeq A^{\prime}\). One thus has \(A^{\prime}\simeq B^{\prime}/IB^{\prime}\); moreover the ring \(B^{\prime}\) is regular; indeed, if \(\mathfrak{m}\) is the maximal ideal of \(B\), \(\mathfrak{m} B^{\prime}\) is the maximal ideal of \(B^{\prime}\), and, since \(\mathfrak{m}\) is generated by a regular sequence by definition of "regular", \(B^{\prime}\) is generated by a \(B^{\prime}\)-regular sequence (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{IV}}\) 15.1.14). Since one evidently has \(\dim B=\dim B^{\prime}\), and since \(I\) and \(IB^{\prime}\) have the same minimal number of generators, the assertion follows.

Theorem 5.6

Let \(A\) be an excellent local ring of characteristic zero. Then one has

\[ \operatorname{prof\;\acute{e}t}(A)\geqslant\operatorname{prof\;g\acute{e}om}(A). \]

One may suppose \(A\) strictly local and complete, since the geometric depth and the étale depth are preserved under passage to the strict henselization and to the completion, according to 5.5 and 1.16. Let \(A\simeq B/I\), where \(B\) is a complete regular local ring, and let \((f_{1},\dots, f_{q})\) be a minimal system of generators of the ideal \(I\). One thus has

\[ \pi=\operatorname{prof\;g\acute{e}om}(A)=\dim B-q. \]

Consider the closed immersion

\[ Y=\operatorname{Spec} A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X=\operatorname{Spec} B, \]

and let \(U=X-Y=\bigcup_{1\leqslant i\leqslant q}X_{f_{i}}\). If \(a\) denotes the closed point of \(X\), one must show that, for every prime number \(p\), one has

\[ \operatorname{H}_{a}^{i}(Y, \mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})=0\quad\textrm{ for }i<\pi. \]

Since \(B\) is regular and excellent, one has \(\operatorname{prof\;\acute{e}t}(B)=2\dim X\) (cf. 1.10) and consequently \(\operatorname{H}_{a}^{i}(X, \mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})=0\) for \(i<2\dim X\). To prove the theorem, it therefore suffices to prove that the morphism

\[ \tag{*} \operatorname{H}_{a}^{i}(X, \mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{a}^{i}(Y, \mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z}) \]

is bijective for \(i<\pi\). For this one applies the local Lefschetz theorem (4.5) with \(n=\pi+q-1\), \(c=q-1\) hence \(n-c=\pi\). Note that \(U=X-Y\) is the union of the \(q\) affine open sets \(X_{f_{i}}\). Let us show that one has, for every point \(u\) of \(U\):

\[ \operatorname{prof\;\acute{e}t}_{u}(X)\geqslant\pi+q-1-\dim\big(\overline{\{ u\} }\big)=\dim\mathscr{O}_{X, u} \]

(where \(\overline{\{ u\} }\) denotes the closure of \(u\) in \(X-\overline{\{ a\} }\)). Indeed it follows from 1.10 that one has

\[ \operatorname{prof\;\acute{e}t}_{u}(X)=2\dim\mathscr{O}_{X, u}\geqslant\dim\mathscr{O}_{X, u}. \]

Using 4.5, one sees that (*) is bijective for \(i<\pi\), which completes the proof of the theorem.

Corollary 5.7

Let \(S\) be the spectrum of a field of characteristic zero (resp. an excellent henselian local scheme of characteristic zero), \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a scheme proper over \(S\) (resp. over \(S-\{ s\}\)). Let \(U\) be a union of \(c+1\) affine open sets of \(X\), \(Y\) a closed subscheme with underlying space \(X-U\), \(n\) and \(m\) integers \(>0\). Suppose that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has

\[ \operatorname{prof\;g\acute{e}om}(\mathscr{O}_{X, u})\geqslant n-\dim\big(\overline{\{ u\} }\big) \]

(\(\overline{\{ u\} }\) closure of \(u\) in \(X\)). Then the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(X, \mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(Y, \mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c-1\).

One applies 4.5 and 4.6. The étale depth hypotheses at the points of \(U\) are verified since one has, according to 5.6

\[ \operatorname{prof\;\acute{e}t}_{u}(X)\geqslant\operatorname{prof\;g\acute{e}om}\mathscr{O}_{X, u}\geqslant n-\dim\big(\overline{\{ u\} }\big). \]

blocks.html: 5 Geometric depth

To apply 4.2 and its corollaries in practice, one must have a convenient criterion allowing one to verify the étale depth hypotheses at the points of \(U\). We shall give such a criterion, using the local Lefschetz theorem (4.5).

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring; when we speak of the étale depth of \(A\), we shall mean the depth at the closed point. We shall introduce a notion of "geometric depth of \(A\)", and use 4.5 to compare it with the étale depth \(\operatorname{prof\;\acute{e}t}(A)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.2

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring; suppose that \(A\) is isomorphic to a quotient of a regular local ring \(B\) by an ideal \(I\) (this holds for example when \(A\) is complete, by virtue of Cohen's theorem (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{IV}}\) 19.8.8)). Let \(q\) be the minimal number of generators of \(I\); then the number \(\dim(B)-q\) is independent of the choice of \(B\).

The minimal number of generators of \(I\) is also equal to the rank of the \(k\)-vector space \(I\otimes_{B}k\), where \(k\) denotes the residue field of \(A\). One reduces at once to the case where \(A\) is complete, for one has \(\widehat{A}\simeq\widehat{B}/\widehat{I}\) with \(\dim \widehat{B}=\dim B\) and \(\operatorname{rg}_{k}(I\otimes_{B}k)=\operatorname{rg}_{k}(\widehat{I}\otimes_{\widehat{B}}k)\); for the same reason one may suppose that the rings \(B\) are complete. Let \(B\) and \(B^{\prime}\) be two complete regular local rings, \(f\colon B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\), \(f^{\prime}\colon B^{\prime}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) two surjective homomorphisms and \(I=\operatorname{Ker}(f)\), \(I^{\prime}=\operatorname{Ker}(f^{\prime})\). One must show that
\[ \dim B-\operatorname{rg}_{k}(I\otimes_{B}k)=\dim B^{\prime}-\operatorname{rg}_{k}(I^{\prime}\otimes_{B^{\prime}}k). \]
Let us first place ourselves in the case where one has a factorization of the form
\[ \xymatrix@C=15pt{ B\ar[rr]^-{f}\ar[rd]_-{g} & & A\\ & B^{\prime}\ar[ru]_-{f^{\prime}} & } \]

with \(g\) surjective. Let \(J=\operatorname{Ker}(g)\); then \(J\subset I\) and \(I/J=I^{\prime}\). Since \(B^{\prime}\) is regular, \(\dim(B^{\prime})=\dim(B)-\operatorname{rg}_{k}(J\otimes_{B}k)\) and \(J\) is generated by elements forming part of a regular system of parameters of \(B\). It follows that one has the exact sequence

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} J\otimes_{B}k\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} I\otimes_{B}k\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} J/I\otimes_{B^{\prime}}k\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]
and consequently

\[ \dim B-\operatorname{rg}_{k}(I\otimes_{B}k)=\dim B-\operatorname{rg}_{k}(J\otimes_{B}k)-\operatorname{rg}_{k}(J/I\otimes_{B^{\prime}}k)=\dim B^{\prime}-\operatorname{rg}_{k}(I^{\prime}\otimes_{B^{\prime}}k). \]

The general case reduces to the preceding one; to see this, it suffices to show that one can find a complete regular local ring \(B^{\prime\prime}\) and surjective homomorphisms \(g\colon B^{\prime\prime}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B\) and \(g^{\prime}\colon B^{\prime\prime}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B^{\prime}\), making the diagram commutative
\[ \tag{*} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix@C=10pt@R=15pt{ & B\ar[rd]^-{f} & \\ B^{\prime\prime}\ar[ru]^-{g}\ar[rd]_-{g^{\prime}} & & A\\ & B^{\prime}.\ar[ru]_-{f^{\prime}} & } \end{array} \]

Now, if \(W\) is a Cohen ring with residue field \(k\), one has a local morphism \(W\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) which lifts to \(B\) and \(B^{\prime}\) (EGA IV 19.8.6), so that one has the commutative diagram
\[ \xymatrix@R=15pt@C=10pt{ & B\ar[rd] & \\ W\ar[ru]\ar[rd] & & A\\ & B^{\prime}.\ar[ru] & } \]

One can find integers \(n\) and \(n^{\prime}\) and surjective morphisms \(h\colon W[\![ T_{1},\dots, T_{n}]\!]\!\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\! B\) and \(h^{\prime}\colon W[\![ T_{1}^{\prime},\dots, T_{n^{\prime}}^{\prime}]\!]\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B^{\prime}\) which are morphisms of \(W\)-algebras (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{IV}}\) 19.8.8); if one then sets \(B^{\prime\prime}=W[\![ T_{1},\dots, T_{n}, T_{1}^{\prime},\dots, T_{n^{\prime}}^{\prime}]\!]\) and if one defines \(g\) and \(g^{\prime}\) as morphisms of \(W\)-algebras such that
\[ g(T_{i})=h(T_{i}), \quad g(T_{i}^{\prime})=b_{i}, \quad g^{\prime}(T_{i})=b_{i}^{\prime}, \quad g^{\prime}(T_{i}^{\prime})=h^{\prime}(T_{i}^{\prime}), \]

where \(b_{i}\) (resp. \(b_{i}^{\prime}\)) is an element of \(B\) (resp. of \(B^{\prime}\)) which lifts \(h^{\prime}.f^{\prime}(T_{i}^{\prime})\) (resp. \(h.f(T_{i})\)), the diagram (*) is indeed commutative.
Proposition 5.2 justifies the following definition:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 5.3

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring, \(\widehat{A}\) its completion, which is therefore isomorphic to the quotient of a complete regular local ring \(B\) by an ideal \(I\); if \(q\) is the minimal number of generators of \(I\), one calls geometric depth of \(A\) the number

\[ \operatorname{prof\;g\acute{e}om}(A)=\dim B-q. \]

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.4

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring. Then one has

\[ \operatorname{prof\;g\acute{e}om}(A)\leqslant\dim A, \]

and one has equality if and only if \(A\) is a complete intersection.

One may suppose \(A\) complete. Let then \(A=B/I\), where \(B\) is a complete regular local ring and \(I\) an ideal of \(B\). If \((x_{1},\dots, x_{q})\) is a minimal system of generators of \(I\), one has \(\dim A\geqslant\dim B-q\), and to say that \(\dim A=\dim B-q\) is equivalent to saying that \((x_{1},\dots, x_{q})\) forms part of a system of parameters of \(B\) (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{IV}}\) 16.3.7); the proposition follows immediately.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.5

Let \(A\) and \(A^{\prime}\) be noetherian local rings, \(f\colon A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A^{\prime}\) a local homomorphism. Suppose that \(f\) is flat and that, denoting by \(k\) the residue field of \(A\), \(A^{\prime}\otimes_{A}k\) is a field, a separable extension of \(K\). Then one has

\[ \operatorname{prof\;g\acute{e}om}(A)=\operatorname{prof\;g\acute{e}om}(A^{\prime}). \]

Up to replacing \(A\) and \(A^{\prime}\) by their completions, one may suppose \(A\) and \(A^{\prime}\) complete (it follows from (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{III}}\) 10.2.1) that the flatness hypothesis is preserved, and this is evident for the other hypotheses). Let then \(A=B/I\), where \(B\) is a regular local ring and \(I\) an ideal of \(B\). Since \(A^{\prime}\) is formally smooth over \(A\) (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{IV}}\) 19.8.2), it follows from (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{IV}}\) 19.7.2) that one can find a complete noetherian local ring \(B^{\prime}\) and a local homomorphism \(B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B^{\prime}\), such that \(B^{\prime}\) is a flat \(B\)-module and such that one has \(B^{\prime}\otimes_{B}A\simeq A^{\prime}\). One thus has \(A^{\prime}\simeq B^{\prime}/IB^{\prime}\); moreover the ring \(B^{\prime}\) is regular; indeed, if \(\mathfrak{m}\) is the maximal ideal of \(B\), \(\mathfrak{m} B^{\prime}\) is the maximal ideal of \(B^{\prime}\), and, since \(\mathfrak{m}\) is generated by a regular sequence by definition of "regular", \(B^{\prime}\) is generated by a \(B^{\prime}\)-regular sequence (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{IV}}\) 15.1.14). Since one evidently has \(\dim B=\dim B^{\prime}\), and since \(I\) and \(IB^{\prime}\) have the same minimal number of generators, the assertion follows.

blocks.title: Theorem 5.6
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.6

Let \(A\) be an excellent local ring of characteristic zero. Then one has

\[ \operatorname{prof\;\acute{e}t}(A)\geqslant\operatorname{prof\;g\acute{e}om}(A). \]

One may suppose \(A\) strictly local and complete, since the geometric depth and the étale depth are preserved under passage to the strict henselization and to the completion, according to 5.5 and 1.16. Let \(A\simeq B/I\), where \(B\) is a complete regular local ring, and let \((f_{1},\dots, f_{q})\) be a minimal system of generators of the ideal \(I\). One thus has
\[ \pi=\operatorname{prof\;g\acute{e}om}(A)=\dim B-q. \]
Consider the closed immersion
\[ Y=\operatorname{Spec} A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X=\operatorname{Spec} B, \]
and let \(U=X-Y=\bigcup_{1\leqslant i\leqslant q}X_{f_{i}}\). If \(a\) denotes the closed point of \(X\), one must show that, for every prime number \(p\), one has
\[ \operatorname{H}_{a}^{i}(Y, \mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})=0\quad\textrm{ for }i<\pi. \]

Since \(B\) is regular and excellent, one has \(\operatorname{prof\;\acute{e}t}(B)=2\dim X\) (cf. 1.10) and consequently \(\operatorname{H}_{a}^{i}(X, \mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})=0\) for \(i<2\dim X\). To prove the theorem, it therefore suffices to prove that the morphism
\[ \tag{*} \operatorname{H}_{a}^{i}(X, \mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{a}^{i}(Y, \mathbf{Z}/p\mathbf{Z}) \]

is bijective for \(i<\pi\). For this one applies the local Lefschetz theorem (4.5) with \(n=\pi+q-1\), \(c=q-1\) hence \(n-c=\pi\). Note that \(U=X-Y\) is the union of the \(q\) affine open sets \(X_{f_{i}}\). Let us show that one has, for every point \(u\) of \(U\):
\[ \operatorname{prof\;\acute{e}t}_{u}(X)\geqslant\pi+q-1-\dim\big(\overline{\{ u\} }\big)=\dim\mathscr{O}_{X, u} \]
(where \(\overline{\{ u\} }\) denotes the closure of \(u\) in \(X-\overline{\{ a\} }\)). Indeed it follows from 1.10 that one has
\[ \operatorname{prof\;\acute{e}t}_{u}(X)=2\dim\mathscr{O}_{X, u}\geqslant\dim\mathscr{O}_{X, u}. \]
Using 4.5, one sees that (*) is bijective for \(i<\pi\), which completes the proof of the theorem.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.7

Let \(S\) be the spectrum of a field of characteristic zero (resp. an excellent henselian local scheme of characteristic zero), \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) a scheme proper over \(S\) (resp. over \(S-\{ s\}\)). Let \(U\) be a union of \(c+1\) affine open sets of \(X\), \(Y\) a closed subscheme with underlying space \(X-U\), \(n\) and \(m\) integers \(>0\). Suppose that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has

\[ \operatorname{prof\;g\acute{e}om}(\mathscr{O}_{X, u})\geqslant n-\dim\big(\overline{\{ u\} }\big) \]

(\(\overline{\{ u\} }\) closure of \(u\) in \(X\)). Then the canonical morphism

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(X, \mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^{i}(Y, \mathbf{Z}/m\mathbf{Z}) \]

is bijective for \(i<n-c-1\), injective for \(i=n-c-1\).
One applies 4.5 and 4.6. The étale depth hypotheses at the points of \(U\) are verified since one has, according to 5.6
\[ \operatorname{prof\;\acute{e}t}_{u}(X)\geqslant\operatorname{prof\;g\acute{e}om}\mathscr{O}_{X, u}\geqslant n-\dim\big(\overline{\{ u\} }\big). \]

pages.title: Open questions
pages.html:
6 Open questions

6.1

One may ask whether the implication (ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i) of 4.2 holds more generally for torsion sheaves \(F\), not necessarily annihilated by a given integer \(m\) and not necessarily constructible. In the case where \(S\) is not of characteristic zero, it seems possible that this implication remains valid, even for sheaves of \(p\)-torsion (\(p\) the residual characteristic). Finally, it is not clear either that the hypothesis \(S\) excellent cannot be removed.

6.2

Let \(X\) be a scheme proper over a field \(k\) or else the complement of the closed point of a henselian local scheme, and let \(j:Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be a closed subscheme of \(X\), whose complement \(U\) is affine. Then, if \(F\) is a sheaf of sets on \(X\) or a sheaf of not necessarily commutative groups, the statements 4.5 or 4.6 and 4.9 still make sense for such an \(F\), provided one restricts to small values of \(n\). If \(u\) is a point of \(U\), one denotes by \(\overline{u}\) a geometric point above \(u\), by \(X(\overline{u})\) the strict localization of \(X\) at \(\overline{u}\) and by \(F_{\overline{u}}\) the stalk of \(F\) at \(\overline{u}\). Then, possibly making certain hypotheses on \(X\) and on \(F\), for example assuming \(X\) excellent (possibly of characteristic zero, or of equal characteristic using resolution of singularities) and \(F\) ind-finite (or if need be even \(\mathbf{L}\)-ind-finite with \(\mathbf{L}\) prime to the characteristic of \(X\)), one would like to prove the following statements:

  1. Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets (resp. a sheaf of groups) and suppose that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has

    \[ F_{\overline{u}} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)\text{ injective if } \dim(\overline{\{u\}}) \leqslant 1 \]

    (that is to say, for such a \(u\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}_u(F) \geqslant 1\)). Then, as \(V\) ranges over the set of open neighbourhoods of \(Y\), the canonical morphism

    \[ \varinjlim_V H^0(V, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(Y, j^*F) \]

    is bijective (resp. one has the preceding conclusion and moreover the morphism \(\varinjlim_V H^1(V, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^1(Y, j^* F)\) is injective). If \(F\) is constructible, one may replace the \(\varinjlim\) by the cohomology of \(V\) for \(V\) "small enough".

  2. Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets (resp. a sheaf of groups) and suppose that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}_u(F) \geqslant 2 -\dim(\overline{\{u\}})\), which also translates into the relations

    \begin{gather*} F_{\overline{u}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)\text{ is bijective if } \dim(\overline{\{u\}})=0\\ F_{\overline{u}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)\text{ is injective if } \dim(\overline{\{u\}})=1. \end{gather*}

    Then the canonical morphism

    \[ H^0(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(Y, j^*F) \]

    is bijective (resp. one has the preceding conclusion and moreover the morphism \(H^1(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^1(Y, j^*F)\) is injective).

  3. Let \(F\) be an ind-finite sheaf of groups. Suppose that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has

    \begin{gather*} F_{\overline{u}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)\text{ bijective if } \dim(\overline{\{u\}})=0\text{ or }1,\\ F_{\overline{u}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)\text{ injective if } \dim(\overline{\{u\}})=2. \end{gather*}

    Then, as \(V\) ranges over the set of open neighbourhoods of \(Y\), the canonical morphisms

    \[ \varinjlim_V H^0(V, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(Y, j^*F) \text{ and } \varinjlim_V H^1(V, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^1(Y, j^*F) \]

    are bijective. If \(F\) is constructible, one may replace the \(\varinjlim\) by the cohomology of \(V\) for \(V\) small enough.

  4. Let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups. Suppose that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}_u(F) \geqslant 3-\dim(\overline{\{u\}})\), which also translates into the conditions

    \begin{gather*} F_{\overline{u}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)\text{ bijective, and } H^1(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)=0 \text{ if } \dim(\overline{\{u\}})=0,\\ F_{\overline{u}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)\text{ bijective if } \dim(\overline{\{u\}})=1,\\ F_{\overline{u}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)\text{ injective if } \dim(\overline{\{u\}})=2. \end{gather*}

    Then the canonical morphisms

    \[ H^0(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(Y, j^*F)\text{ and } H^1(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^1(Y, j^*F) \]

    are bijective.

As an indication in favour of these statements, let us point out XIII 2.1, X 3.4 and XII 3.5. Let us note that, thanks to the argument of 4.8 and 4.9, statement a) (resp. c)) would follow from b) (resp. d)).

6.3

From d) there would follow the following statement analogous to 5.6: if \(A\) is a noetherian local ring (possibly excellent) and if one has \(\operatorname{prof} \operatorname{g\acute{e}om} (A) \geqslant 3\), then one has \(\operatorname{prof} \operatorname{hop} (A) \geqslant 3\). To see this, one realizes \(Y'=\operatorname{Spec} A\) as a closed subset of a regular local scheme \(X'=\operatorname{Spec} B\), whose complement is a union of \(q\) affine open sets, with the relation \(\dim B -q=\operatorname{prof} \operatorname{g\acute{e}om}(A)\). One has, for every point \(x\) of \(X'\), if \(n=\dim B\), \(\operatorname{prof} \operatorname{hop}_x(X) \geqslant \inf(3, n-\dim(\overline{\{x\}}))\) (cf. 1.11) and one deduces from d) that this implies \(\operatorname{prof} \operatorname{hop}_y(Y') \geqslant \inf(3, n-q-\dim(\overline{\{y\}}))\), for every point \(y\) of \(Y'\). The result is then obtained by taking for \(y\) the closed point of \(Y'\).

6.4

A variant of 4.2, at least of the implication (ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i), ought still to be valid in the complex analytic case, provided one works with "analytically constructible" sheaves (cf. XIII); the proof would be analogous to that of 4.2, using the duality theory of J.-L. Verdier. Note on the other hand that, for the complex analytic analogue of the non-commutative variants pointed out in 6.2, one does not even have a method of attack for the statements concerning the fundamental group suggested by the results of Exposés X, XII, XIII, recalled at the end of 6.2. The methods of the Seminar indeed seem irremediably tied to the case of finite coverings (which can be studied in terms of coherent sheaves of Algebras).

[1]
M. Artin and B. Mazur, Homotopy of Varieties in Etale Topology, in Proceedings of a Conference on Local Fields, Springer (1967). ↩
[2]
J.-P. Serre, Cohomologie Galoisienne, Springer-Verlag, 1964. ↩
[3]
J.-L. Verdier, Algèbre homologique et Catégories dérivées, North. Holland Pub. Cie. à parître ↩

blocks.html: 6 Open questions

One may ask whether the implication (ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i) of 4.2 holds more generally for torsion sheaves \(F\), not necessarily annihilated by a given integer \(m\) and not necessarily constructible. In the case where \(S\) is not of characteristic zero, it seems possible that this implication remains valid, even for sheaves of \(p\)-torsion (\(p\) the residual characteristic). Finally, it is not clear either that the hypothesis \(S\) excellent cannot be removed.

Let \(X\) be a scheme proper over a field \(k\) or else the complement of the closed point of a henselian local scheme, and let \(j:Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be a closed subscheme of \(X\), whose complement \(U\) is affine. Then, if \(F\) is a sheaf of sets on \(X\) or a sheaf of not necessarily commutative groups, the statements 4.5 or 4.6 and 4.9 still make sense for such an \(F\), provided one restricts to small values of \(n\). If \(u\) is a point of \(U\), one denotes by \(\overline{u}\) a geometric point above \(u\), by \(X(\overline{u})\) the strict localization of \(X\) at \(\overline{u}\) and by \(F_{\overline{u}}\) the stalk of \(F\) at \(\overline{u}\). Then, possibly making certain hypotheses on \(X\) and on \(F\), for example assuming \(X\) excellent (possibly of characteristic zero, or of equal characteristic using resolution of singularities) and \(F\) ind-finite (or if need be even \(\mathbf{L}\)-ind-finite with \(\mathbf{L}\) prime to the characteristic of \(X\)), one would like to prove the following statements:

1. Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets (resp. a sheaf of groups) and suppose that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has

    \[ F_{\overline{u}} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)\text{ injective if } \dim(\overline{\{u\}}) \leqslant 1 \]

    (that is to say, for such a \(u\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}_u(F) \geqslant 1\)). Then, as \(V\) ranges over the set of open neighbourhoods of \(Y\), the canonical morphism

    \[ \varinjlim_V H^0(V, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(Y, j^*F) \]

    is bijective (resp. one has the preceding conclusion and moreover the morphism \(\varinjlim_V H^1(V, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^1(Y, j^* F)\) is injective). If \(F\) is constructible, one may replace the \(\varinjlim\) by the cohomology of \(V\) for \(V\) "small enough".

  2. Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets (resp. a sheaf of groups) and suppose that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}_u(F) \geqslant 2 -\dim(\overline{\{u\}})\), which also translates into the relations

    \begin{gather*} F_{\overline{u}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)\text{ is bijective if } \dim(\overline{\{u\}})=0\\ F_{\overline{u}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)\text{ is injective if } \dim(\overline{\{u\}})=1. \end{gather*}

    Then the canonical morphism

    \[ H^0(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(Y, j^*F) \]

    is bijective (resp. one has the preceding conclusion and moreover the morphism \(H^1(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^1(Y, j^*F)\) is injective).

  3. Let \(F\) be an ind-finite sheaf of groups. Suppose that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has

    \begin{gather*} F_{\overline{u}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)\text{ bijective if } \dim(\overline{\{u\}})=0\text{ or }1,\\ F_{\overline{u}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)\text{ injective if } \dim(\overline{\{u\}})=2. \end{gather*}

    Then, as \(V\) ranges over the set of open neighbourhoods of \(Y\), the canonical morphisms

    \[ \varinjlim_V H^0(V, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(Y, j^*F) \text{ and } \varinjlim_V H^1(V, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^1(Y, j^*F) \]

    are bijective. If \(F\) is constructible, one may replace the \(\varinjlim\) by the cohomology of \(V\) for \(V\) small enough.

  4. Let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups. Suppose that, for every point \(u\) of \(U\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}_u(F) \geqslant 3-\dim(\overline{\{u\}})\), which also translates into the conditions

    \begin{gather*} F_{\overline{u}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)\text{ bijective, and } H^1(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)=0 \text{ if } \dim(\overline{\{u\}})=0,\\ F_{\overline{u}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)\text{ bijective if } \dim(\overline{\{u\}})=1,\\ F_{\overline{u}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(X(\overline{u})-\overline{u}, F)\text{ injective if } \dim(\overline{\{u\}})=2. \end{gather*}

    Then the canonical morphisms

    \[ H^0(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^0(Y, j^*F)\text{ and } H^1(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^1(Y, j^*F) \]

    are bijective.

As an indication in favour of these statements, let us point out XIII 2.1, X 3.4 and XII 3.5. Let us note that, thanks to the argument of 4.8 and 4.9, statement a) (resp. c)) would follow from b) (resp. d)).

From d) there would follow the following statement analogous to 5.6: if \(A\) is a noetherian local ring (possibly excellent) and if one has \(\operatorname{prof} \operatorname{g\acute{e}om} (A) \geqslant 3\), then one has \(\operatorname{prof} \operatorname{hop} (A) \geqslant 3\). To see this, one realizes \(Y'=\operatorname{Spec} A\) as a closed subset of a regular local scheme \(X'=\operatorname{Spec} B\), whose complement is a union of \(q\) affine open sets, with the relation \(\dim B -q=\operatorname{prof} \operatorname{g\acute{e}om}(A)\). One has, for every point \(x\) of \(X'\), if \(n=\dim B\), \(\operatorname{prof} \operatorname{hop}_x(X) \geqslant \inf(3, n-\dim(\overline{\{x\}}))\) (cf. 1.11) and one deduces from d) that this implies \(\operatorname{prof} \operatorname{hop}_y(Y') \geqslant \inf(3, n-q-\dim(\overline{\{y\}}))\), for every point \(y\) of \(Y'\). The result is then obtained by taking for \(y\) the closed point of \(Y'\).

A variant of 4.2, at least of the implication (ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (i), ought still to be valid in the complex analytic case, provided one works with "analytically constructible" sheaves (cf. XIII); the proof would be analogous to that of 4.2, using the duality theory of J.-L. Verdier. Note on the other hand that, for the complex analytic analogue of the non-commutative variants pointed out in 6.2, one does not even have a method of attack for the statements concerning the fundamental group suggested by the results of Exposés X, XII, XIII, recalled at the end of 6.2. The methods of the Seminar indeed seem irremediably tied to the case of finite coverings (which can be studied in terms of coherent sheaves of Algebras).
